Dr. Charrier is originally from Central Maine and has called the Tampa area home since 2000. Dr. Charrier specializes in comprehensive hearing evaluations, hearing aid selection and fittings, and provides supportive rehabilitative counseling to her patients.

data.meta_title: Are Cheap Hearing Aids Worth Buying? | Audiology & Hearing Centers
data.meta_description: Are cheap hearing aids worth buying? Learn the risks, benefits, and safer options. Get expert guidance. Schedule a hearing evaluation today.
data.content:
• Cheap hearing aids may seem appealing, but they often lack the testing, sound quality, and support needed for real hearing improvement.
  • Professional hearing aids come with expert fitting, adjustment, and long-term care that make everyday listening safer and more comfortable.
  • Knowing the differences between low-cost devices and professionally fitted hearing aids helps you make the best choice for your hearing health.

Many people struggling with hearing loss begin their search by wondering, are cheap hearing aids worth buying? These devices are easy to find online, at drugstores, and on shopping websites. Their low price can be tempting, especially if you are trying to save money or want a quick fix. But hearing loss is more complex than most people realize, and choosing the wrong device can lead to frustration, wasted money, and even worse hearing over time.

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we help patients understand the real differences between low-cost hearing devices and high-quality hearing aids that are tested, programmed, and supported by professionals. Before you spend money on a cheap device, it’s important to know what you’re actually getting and what you may be missing.

What Are Cheap Hearing Aids?

Cheap hearing aids are low-cost devices that claim to make sounds louder. They are often priced under $100 or even under $50. These devices may include:

  • Online “hearing aids” with generic volume buttons
  • Small amplifiers claiming to help with hearing
  • Basic devices sold in pharmacies or mail-order catalogs

Many of these low-priced options are not true hearing aids. Instead, they are personal sound amplification products (PSAPs), devices meant to make all sounds louder, not improve hearing safely or correctly.

While cheap hearing aids may seem convenient, they rarely provide the clarity, comfort, or safety that a person with hearing loss needs.

Why Price Alone Doesn’t Tell the Full Story

Low-cost hearing devices may seem like a great deal, but they are often cheap for a reason. They do not go through the same testing, medical-grade development, or quality checks as real hearing aids. They are not designed for personalized hearing needs, and they rarely come with support if something goes wrong.

Professional hearing aids cost more because they include:

  • Advanced sound processing
  • Medical-grade parts
  • Custom programming based on your hearing test
  • Noise management
  • Feedback control
  • Long-term support
  • Warranty and repair services

Hearing loss is not simply “everything sounding quiet.” It involves changes in how your ears and brain process sound. Cheap devices cannot correct these changes. They only make everything louder.

Audiology and Hearing Centers offers a wide selection of hearing aids in Tampa Westchase, FL and other key locations.

Hearing Loss Needs More Than Volume

Many people believe hearing loss is solved by turning the volume up. But real hearing loss is not just about loudness. It affects:

  • Speech understanding
  • Word clarity
  • Background noise filtering
  • Directional listening
  • How the brain interprets sound

Cheap hearing aids and amplifiers do not adjust sound based on these needs. They simply boost every noise equally including voices, background sounds, traffic, wind, and more. This often makes hearing even more difficult.

Professionally programmed hearing aids, on the other hand:

  • Boost specific frequencies you struggle with
  • Lower loud or harsh sounds
  • Reduce background noise
  • Improve speech clarity
  • Support how the brain processes sound

This is why getting the right device (and not just a cheap one) makes such a big difference.

Benefits of Professionally Fitted Hearing Aids

Buying hearing aids from a professional comes with many advantages that cheap devices cannot match.

1. Precise Hearing Testing

Before recommending a hearing aid, an audiologist performs a full hearing evaluation that tests many parts of hearing, including:

  • Different sound levels
  • Different pitches
  • Speech understanding
  • How your ears and brain respond to sound

This detailed information helps determine what kind of help you need. Cheap devices cannot offer this level of precision.

2. Custom Programming

Professional hearing aids are programmed based on your exact hearing results. Your provider adjusts:

  • Volume
  • Frequency settings
  • Background noise controls
  • Listening comfort levels
  • Speech clarity tools

Cheap hearing aids have no customization. They use one setting for everyone.

3. Comfort and Fit

Professionally fitted hearing aids sit comfortably in the ear and are less likely to cause irritation, slipping, or pain. Cheap devices often use stiff, poorly fitting tips that can:

  • Cause soreness
  • Press on the ear canal
  • Whistle or give feedback
  • Fall out easily

Comfort is a major part of hearing aid success.

4. Better Sound Quality

Professional hearing aids include smart technology such as:

  • Directional microphones
  • Digital signal processing
  • Feedback management
  • Bluetooth connectivity
  • Automatic adjustments
  • Noise reduction

Cheap devices cannot match these features.

5. Ongoing Care and Support

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, your care does not end once you receive your hearing aids. We provide:

  • Cleaning
  • Repairs
  • Fine-tuning
  • Follow-up visits
  • Earwax checks
  • Education and training

Cheap hearing aids come with no real support. If something goes wrong, you’re on your own.

Risks of Using Cheap Hearing Aids

While cheap hearing aids may seem harmless, they can actually cause serious problems.

Risk #1: Making Hearing Loss Worse

Too much volume or the wrong frequencies can damage the ear further.

Risk #2: Missing Medical Warning Signs

Some types of hearing loss need medical care. Cheap devices hide these signs instead of identifying them.

Risk #3: Unsafe or Poorly Built Components

Low-cost devices may include materials that irritate the ear or break easily.

Risk #4: Wasted Money

Even at a low price, devices that don’t work are still a waste of money. Many patients end up stopping use quickly.

Risk #5: Delayed Treatment

Trying cheap devices first can delay getting the help you truly need.

Who Might Benefit from a Cheap Device?

There are only a few limited cases where a cheap device might be useful:

  • Someone needing a temporary device
  • Someone wanting to try amplification before a full evaluation
  • Someone with very mild hearing difficulty who does not want a medical-grade aid yet

Even then, it is still recommended to get a hearing screening first.

How OTC Hearing Aids Compare

Over-the-counter hearing aids (OTC), which are now FDA-regulated, fall somewhere between cheap devices and professional hearing aids. They are meant for adults with mild to moderate hearing loss.

OTC hearing aids may be helpful if:

  • Your hearing loss is mild
  • You are comfortable adjusting settings yourself
  • You understand they are not custom-fit

Even with OTC hearing aids, having a hearing evaluation at an audiology clinic helps ensure you choose the right device.

When Cheap Hearing Aids Fail, What Happens Next?

Many people try cheap hearing aids first and then come to Audiology & Hearing Centers after feeling disappointed or overwhelmed.

Common reasons include:

  • Loud, harsh sound
  • Difficulty hearing voices
  • Pain or discomfort
  • Whistling or squealing
  • No improvement in real-world listening
  • Short battery life
  • No support when issues arise

Once a person tries a professionally fitted hearing aid, the difference is often immediate and surprising.

Why Professional Care Matters

Hearing loss affects communication, relationships, confidence, and safety. This is why hearing healthcare should never be rushed or treated like an online purchase.

Audiologists and hearing specialists offer:

  • Accurate diagnosis
  • Guidance based on real data
  • Device options that match your needs
  • Follow-up care that supports long-term success

Cheap hearing aids cannot provide this level of attention or expertise.

Are Cheap Hearing Aids Worth Buying?

So, are cheap hearing aids worth buying? For most people, the answer is no. They may offer a short-term boost in loudness, but they do not provide safe, clear, or lasting hearing support.

When it comes to hearing, quality matters. Professional evaluation, proper programming, and ongoing care lead to better results, greater comfort, and a happier life.

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we help patients find hearing solutions that match their lifestyle, hearing loss, and long-term goals, not just their budget.

Better Hearing Starts With a Hearing Evaluation

Before you buy any hearing device, it’s always best to start with a hearing test. This gives you clear answers and helps you choose the safest and most effective option.

Audiology & Hearing Centers offer complete hearing care and hearing aids in:

  • Alpharetta, GA
  • Bonita Springs, FL
  • Ocala, FL
  • Palm Harbor, FL
  • Sarasota, FL
  • Tampa Palms, FL
  • Tampa Westchase, FL
  • Venice, FL

Let us help you hear clearly, confidently, and comfortably.

Schedule an Appointment Today

If you are still asking are cheap hearing aids worth buying, remember this:

Cheap devices may seem like a bargain, but they rarely help in the long term. Professional hearing aids, fit, adjusted, and supported by experts, offer the best hearing results and the best quality of life.

data.meta_title: Audiology & Hearing Center | Audiology Services
data.meta_description: Learn more about our personalized hearing care, from comprehensive evaluations to advanced hearing aid solutions in Tampa Bay & Venice, FL.
data.content:
At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we’re dedicated to helping you hear your best. Our expert audiologists provide personalized hearing care, from comprehensive evaluations to advanced hearing aid solutions. Whether you’re experiencing hearing loss, tinnitus, or simply want to protect your hearing, we’re here to guide you every step of the way. 

With compassionate care and the latest technology, we make better hearing simple and stress-free. Visit us today and take the first step toward clearer, more confident listening.

Hearing Services in Tampa Palms, Venice, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Sarasota, Bonita Springs, Naples, Ocala FL and Alpharetta, GA

Hearing Evaluations

A comprehensive hearing evaluation helps identify hearing loss and determine the best solutions for your needs. Our audiologists use advanced testing to assess your hearing and recommend personalized treatment options.

Which hearing test is the best?

The best hearing test depends on your specific needs. A comprehensive hearing evaluation conducted by an audiologist is the most accurate way to assess your hearing. This evaluation typically includes:

  • Pure-tone audiometry – Determines the softest sounds you can hear at different pitches.
  • Speech audiometry – Measures how well you hear and understand speech.
  • Tympanometry – Evaluates middle ear function, checking for issues like fluid buildup or infections.

If you’re experiencing hearing difficulties, a comprehensive hearing evaluation is the most reliable way to get an accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment.

Can I test my own hearing?

While you can try to assess your hearing on your own, self-testing is not as accurate as a professional evaluation. You might notice signs of hearing loss, such as struggling to hear in noisy settings or turning up the volume on the TV. Some smartphone apps and online tests can give you a general idea of your hearing ability, but they cannot diagnose hearing loss or its cause. If you suspect a problem, it’s best to see an audiologist for a thorough assessment.

Are online hearing tests any good?

Online hearing tests can be a helpful first step in checking for hearing loss, but they have limitations. These tests typically measure how well you hear tones at different frequencies using headphones or speakers. However, they cannot replace a professional evaluation, as they do not assess speech understanding, middle ear function, or the underlying cause of hearing loss.

If an online test suggests hearing difficulties, scheduling a full hearing evaluation with an audiologist is the best way to get an accurate diagnosis and personalized hearing solutions.

Diagnostic Services

We offer a range of diagnostic services to assess hearing health and detect any underlying conditions. Our team ensures accurate results, guiding you toward the best treatment plan.

What are diagnostic hearing tests, and why are they important?

Diagnostic hearing tests are specialized evaluations that assess your hearing ability and detect any underlying conditions affecting your ears. These tests help determine the type, severity, and cause of hearing loss, allowing audiologists to recommend the best treatment options, whether it’s hearing aids, medical intervention, or other hearing solutions.

What types of diagnostic hearing tests are performed?

Audiologists use a variety of tests to assess hearing health, including:

  • Pure-tone audiometry – Measures your ability to hear different tones at various pitches and volumes.
  • Speech audiometry – Evaluates how well you hear and understand spoken words.
  • Tympanometry – Checks middle ear function to detect issues like fluid buildup or eardrum problems.
  • Otoacoustic emissions (OAE) testing – Assesses inner ear function, often used for newborns and young children.
  • Auditory brainstem response (ABR) testing – Evaluates how the brain processes sound, often used for neurological assessments.

When should I get a diagnostic hearing test?

You should consider a diagnostic hearing test if you experience:

  • Difficulty hearing conversations, especially in noisy environments
  • Frequent ringing or buzzing in your ears (tinnitus)
  • Muffled sounds or a sensation of fullness in the ears
  • Sudden or gradual hearing loss
  • Dizziness or balance issues

If you notice any of these symptoms, scheduling a hearing evaluation with an audiologist can help identify potential hearing problems and guide you toward the best treatment options.

Tinnitus Evaluations and Treatment

Tinnitus, or ringing in the ears, can be frustrating and disruptive. Our tinnitus evaluations help pinpoint the cause, and our treatment options, including sound therapy and management techniques, can help you find relief.

Should I go to an audiologist for tinnitus?

Yes, an audiologist is one of the best healthcare professionals to assess and manage tinnitus. Tinnitus (ringing or buzzing in the ears) can be linked to hearing loss, noise exposure, ear infections, or other medical conditions. An audiologist can conduct a tinnitus evaluation, identify potential causes, and recommend treatments such as:

  • Sound therapy to help mask or reduce the perception of tinnitus
  • Hearing aids with built-in tinnitus management features
  • Counseling and coping strategies to minimize tinnitus-related stress
  • Referrals to medical specialists if additional treatment is needed

If tinnitus is affecting your daily life, seeing an audiologist can help you find relief and better manage your symptoms.

Is tinnitus a brain problem?

While tinnitus is often linked to the ears, research suggests it is actually a neurological issue involving the brain’s auditory system. When the brain no longer receives certain sound signals due to hearing loss or ear damage, it may compensate by creating its own noise—resulting in the ringing or buzzing sensation known as tinnitus.

Because tinnitus involves the brain’s interpretation of sound, treatment focuses on managing perception rather than eliminating the noise itself. This is why sound therapy, cognitive retraining, and stress management techniques can be effective in reducing tinnitus symptoms.

What happens if tinnitus is not treated?

While tinnitus itself is not life-threatening, ignoring it can lead to other complications, including:

  • Increased stress and anxiety due to persistent noise
  • Difficulty concentrating or sleeping
  • Worsening hearing problems if related hearing loss is left unaddressed
  • Emotional distress, including frustration, depression, or social withdrawal

Without treatment, tinnitus may become more noticeable and disruptive over time. Seeking professional help can improve your quality of life and prevent tinnitus from interfering with daily activities.

Ear Wax Removal

Blocked ears can affect hearing and cause discomfort. Our safe and professional ear wax removal services help clear your ears, improving your hearing and overall ear health.

What is the best way to remove ear wax?

The safest way to remove ear wax is through professional ear cleaning by an audiologist or healthcare provider. If you need to clear minor buildup at home, consider these safe methods:

✔ Over-the-counter ear drops – Softens wax so it can naturally exit the ear.
✔ Warm water rinse – Using a bulb syringe with lukewarm water can help flush out softened wax.
✔ Microsuction or irrigation (by a professional) – Performed by an audiologist or doctor for excessive buildup.

Avoid cotton swabs, bobby pins, or other objects, as they can push wax deeper into the ear and cause blockages or damage.

Do ear candles work?

No, ear candles are not a safe or effective method for ear wax removal. Studies show that they do not create enough suction to pull out wax and can actually cause burns, blockages, or injury to the ear canal. If you’re dealing with excessive wax, it’s best to use safer removal methods or see a professional for proper cleaning.

What does dark brown ear wax mean?

Ear wax color can vary based on age, environment, and ear health. Dark brown or black ear wax is usually older wax that has been exposed to air and oxidized. It may also be more common in individuals with higher ear wax production. In some cases, very dark ear wax could indicate a buildup that needs removal.

However, if you experience pain, drainage, or a sudden change in ear wax appearance, it’s best to consult an audiologist or doctor to rule out infection or other concerns.

Hearing Aid Services

From choosing the right hearing aid to ongoing support, we provide expert care to help you get the most out of your device. Our team ensures proper fitting, adjustments, repairs, and long-term maintenance.

Hearing Aid Fittings

A properly fitted hearing aid makes all the difference. We customize your device for comfort and optimal performance, ensuring a natural listening experience tailored to your needs.

How long does it take to get fitted for a hearing aid?

The hearing aid fitting process typically takes about one to two hours. During this appointment, the audiologist will adjust the hearing aids to match your specific hearing needs, ensure a proper fit, and provide instructions on how to use and care for them. If custom earmolds are needed, it may take an additional one to two weeks for manufacturing before the final fitting.

How does an audiologist fit a hearing aid?

An audiologist fits a hearing aid by:

  1. Programming the device – Using your hearing test results, the audiologist adjusts the settings to match your level of hearing loss.
  2. Ensuring comfort and fit – The hearing aid is placed in your ear and adjusted for a secure and comfortable fit.
  3. Real-ear measurement (REM) – This test ensures that the hearing aid amplifies sounds correctly by measuring sound levels inside your ear canal.
  4. Teaching proper use and care – The audiologist will explain how to wear, clean, and maintain the hearing aids for the best performance.
  5. Making adjustments as needed – Follow-up appointments may be scheduled to fine-tune the settings based on your experience.

Can you get a hearing aid without a test?

While some over-the-counter (OTC) hearing aids are available without a professional test, getting a comprehensive hearing evaluation from an audiologist ensures that you receive the right device for your specific needs. A professional test can determine the type and severity of hearing loss, ensuring that you get properly programmed and well-fitted hearing aids for the best results. If you have underlying medical conditions affecting your hearing, an audiologist can also provide guidance on appropriate treatment options.

Hearing Aid Repair

If your hearing aid isn’t working as it should, we offer fast and reliable repairs to restore its functionality. Whether it’s a simple fix or a more complex issue, we can help. Contact us to learn more about our hearing aid repair services.

What are common problems that require hearing aid repair?

Hearing aids can experience various issues over time. Some of the most common problems include:

  • No sound or weak volume – Could be caused by a clogged wax filter, dead battery, or internal damage.
  • Distorted or muffled sound – May indicate moisture buildup, a damaged microphone, or issues with the receiver.
  • Whistling or feedback noise – Often due to improper fit, earwax buildup, or malfunctioning components.
  • Physical damage – Cracks, broken battery doors, or loose parts may need professional repair.

How long does a hearing aid repair take?

The time required for a repair depends on the issue. Minor repairs, such as cleaning or replacing a wax filter, can often be done in the clinic within a few minutes. More complex repairs, like fixing internal components or replacing broken parts, may require the hearing aid to be sent to the manufacturer, which can take several days to a couple of weeks.

Can I fix my hearing aid at home?

Some minor hearing aid issues can be resolved at home, such as:
✔ Replacing the battery
✔ Cleaning the microphone and wax filter
✔ Checking for moisture and using a hearing aid dehumidifier
✔ Ensuring the volume is correctly adjusted

However, if the hearing aid is still not working properly, do not attempt to open or repair internal components yourself. This could cause further damage. Instead, contact your audiologist or hearing aid provider for professional repair services.

data.meta_title: Best Way to Clean Ears | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Learn the safest, doctor-recommended ways to handle earwax from expert audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL.
data.content:
Cleaning your ears might seem like part of your regular hygiene routine, but did you know your ears usually clean themselves? Many people reach for cotton swabs or other objects to remove earwax, but this can do more harm than good. 

At Audiology & Hearing Center in Westchase, FL, we understand the importance of healthy ears and safe earwax removal. Our experienced team offers professional ear cleaning and audiology services to help you hear your best without risking damage. 

Before you grab that Q-tip, keep reading to learn the safest and most effective ways to care for your ears—and what to avoid.

The Logic Behind Ear Wax

Ear wax, or cerumen, might seem like something to get rid of, but it actually serves an important purpose. Produced by glands in the ear canal, earwax helps trap dust, dirt, and other small particles, preventing them from reaching and damaging the eardrum. It also has antibacterial properties and helps keep the skin inside your ears moisturized, protecting against dryness and irritation.

The body naturally moves old ear wax out of the ear canal when you chew, talk, or move your jaw. But many people try to clean their ears by inserting cotton swabs, hairpins, or other objects into their ears. This can push wax deeper, causing a blockage, irritation, or even injury. In severe cases, using sharp tools can damage the eardrum or ear canal lining, leading to hearing loss or infection. That’s why it’s crucial to let ear wax do its job—or have it removed safely by a professional when needed.

Audiology & Hearing Center provides professional ear wax removal in Westchase, FL.

Best Way to Clean Ears: Should You Clean Your Ears?

In most cases, you don’t need to clean your ears at all. Your ears are designed to self-clean. However, some people naturally produce more wax or may experience a buildup that doesn’t clear on its own. This is called cerumen impaction, and it can cause symptoms like ear fullness, itching, hearing loss, ringing (tinnitus), dizziness, or ear pain.

If you notice any of these symptoms, do not try to fix the issue at home. See a hearing professional or audiologist. 

At Audiology & Hearing Center, our expert Westchase, FL audiologists can safely remove ear wax using tools and methods that are gentle and effective—without the risks that come from DIY cleaning.

Cerumen Impaction and Its Symptoms

Cerumen impaction happens when earwax builds up to the point of blocking the ear canal. This can happen from naturally heavy wax production, narrow ear canals, or using objects like Q-tips that push wax deeper.

Common symptoms include:

  • A feeling of fullness in the ear
  • Muffled or reduced hearing
  • Ringing or buzzing sounds (tinnitus)
  • Dizziness or balance issues
  • Discomfort or pain in the ear

If you’re experiencing any of these, it’s time to book an appointment. Our trained audiologists can help you find relief safely.

Ear Cleaning Do’s and Don’ts

Do:

  • Let your ears clean themselves if there’s no discomfort
  • Use a warm, damp washcloth to wipe the outside of the ear
  • Use doctor-recommended ear drops if needed
  • See an audiologist or ENT for regular ear health checkups
  • Schedule professional ear wax removal if you’re prone to buildup

Don’t:

  • Insert cotton swabs, fingers, or any objects into your ears
  • Use candles, suction kits, or DIY irrigation systems without guidance
  • Ignore symptoms like pain, pressure, or hearing changes
  • Try to remove impacted wax at home—it could make things worse

What is the best way to remove ear wax?

The safest method is professional ear wax removal done by an audiologist. Options may include softening the wax with drops, suctioning, or using specialized tools to gently remove it. Over-the-counter wax removal kits can be helpful, but should only be used under a professional’s guidance.

Bottom Line

Ear wax plays an essential role in protecting your ears, and most of the time, your body takes care of it for you. But when buildup causes discomfort, hearing problems, or other symptoms, professional help is the best solution. Avoid poking or inserting anything into your ears—it often does more harm than good. 

Trust the experts at Audiology & Hearing Center in Westchase, FL, for safe, comfortable care when you need it.

Professional Ear Wax Removal in Westchase, FL

If you’re dealing with ear wax buildup or want to learn more about ear health, schedule an appointment with Audiology & Hearing Center.

We provide professional ear wax removal in Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Sarasota, Bonita Springs FL and Alpharetta, GA. 

Our audiologists are here to help you hear clearly and safely. We also have clinics located in Tampa Palms, FL, Palm Harbor, FL, and Venice, FL.

data.meta_description: Struggling with ringing in your ears? Discover how hearing aids can help manage tinnitus symptoms. Visit Audiology and Hearing Center in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL for expert care.
data.content:
If you’ve ever heard a ringing, buzzing, or hissing sound in your ears when there’s no actual noise around, you may be experiencing tinnitus. This condition affects millions of Americans and can range from a mild nuisance to a daily challenge that interferes with sleep, focus, and emotional well-being.

At Audiology and Hearing Center, our team of experienced audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL, understands how distressing tinnitus can be. 

One of the most effective ways we help patients manage tinnitus is through the use of hearing aids, especially when hearing loss is also present.

Let’s explore how hearing aids for tinnitus can help reduce symptoms and why it’s essential to see a licensed audiologist for a personalized care plan.

First things first – 

What Is Tinnitus?

Tinnitus is the perception of sound when no external sound is present. It is often described as:

  • Ringing
  • Buzzing
  • Roaring
  • Clicking
  • Hissing
  • Musical

Tinnitus can be constant or come and go, and it can affect one or both ears. While tinnitus itself is not a disease, it is often a symptom of an underlying issue, commonly hearing loss, ear injury, or problems with the auditory nerve.

How Hearing Loss and Tinnitus Are Connected

Many people with tinnitus also have some degree of hearing loss, often without realizing it. When the brain receives fewer sound signals from the ears (due to hearing loss), it sometimes “fills in the blanks” by generating phantom sounds—what we know as tinnitus.

Because of this connection, treating hearing loss can often reduce or even eliminate tinnitus symptoms. That’s where hearing aids come in.

How Hearing Aids For Tinnitus Can Help 

Modern hearing aids are more than just devices for making sounds louder. Many are now equipped with tinnitus therapy features specifically designed to help manage and minimize the symptoms of tinnitus.

Here are a few ways hearing aids can help:

1. Restore Hearing Input

Hearing aids amplify external sounds, helping to mask or reduce the awareness of internal tinnitus sounds. When you can hear better, you may notice the ringing less.

2. Sound Therapy Options

Some advanced hearing aids offer built-in sound therapy programs. These features play soothing sounds—such as ocean waves, white noise, or soft tones—to help distract your brain from the tinnitus.

3. Reduce Listening Fatigue

Hearing loss forces your brain to work harder to understand speech and sounds. This mental strain can worsen tinnitus. Hearing aids relieve that strain, which can make tinnitus less bothersome.

4. Better Sleep and Focus

By improving hearing and reducing tinnitus, patients often sleep better and feel more focused throughout the day—key to improving quality of life.

Why You Should See an Audiologist

Not all hearing aids are created equal, and not every case of tinnitus is the same. A thorough evaluation by a licensed audiologist is the first step toward relief. At Audiology and Hearing Center, we:

  • Conduct comprehensive hearing and tinnitus assessments
  • Customize hearing aid fittings with tinnitus relief features
  • Offer counseling and education on tinnitus management
  • Provide ongoing support to help you adapt to new technology

Trying to self-treat tinnitus with over-the-counter devices or general sound apps may not give you the results you need. 

Our Tampa Bay, FL audiologists tailor solutions based on your hearing profile and personal comfort, ensuring the best chance for success.

Hearing Aids in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL

If tinnitus is disrupting your life, you don’t have to suffer in silence. Hearing aids may be the key to finding relief, especially when they’re paired with expert guidance and care.

At Audiology and Hearing Center, we’ve helped countless patients rediscover the joy of better hearing and quieter minds. Let us help you, too!

Schedule a consultation today and take the first step toward effective tinnitus management with a compassionate team that truly listens.

We provide a wide selection of hearing aids in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL.

Our clinics are located in Tampa Palms, FL, Tampa Westchase, FL, Palm Harbor, FL, and Venice, FL.

data.meta_description: Curious about Costco Hearing Aids? Learn what they are, how they work, and why visiting an audiologist is the best step for your hearing health.
data.content:
Costco has become a popular place for people to buy hearing aids. Known for bulk shopping and savings, Costco also offers hearing tests and sells hearing aids in their stores. Over the years, they’ve added hearing centers in many locations. You don’t need to be a Costco member to get your hearing tested, but you do need a membership to buy hearing aids.

Costco offers several well-known brands and has its own line of hearing aids called Kirkland Signature. Many people are drawn to Costco because of the lower prices and the convenience of shopping while running other errands.

But hearing loss is a medical issue. That’s why it’s so important to get tested and fitted by a licensed audiologist. At Audiology & Hearing Centers,  we provide expert care that goes far beyond the basics. 

We connect you to expert audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL,

Before you decide where to buy your hearing aids, let’s take a closer look at what Costco offers—and what they don’t.

What Hearing Aids Are Available at Costco?

Costco sells hearing aids from several major companies. These include:

  • Kirkland Signature (made by Sonova)
  • Jabra Enhance (made by ReSound)
  • Rexton (part of the Signia/Siemens family)
  • Philips HearLink

These hearing aids include features like Bluetooth streaming, rechargeable batteries, and smartphone apps. They’re often priced lower than similar models sold in clinics.

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we carry some of the same trusted brands, such as:

  • Phonak
  • ReSound
  • Widex
  • Oticon
  • Starkey
  • Unitron
  • Signia

But when you come to us, you also get a full hearing evaluation and a professional fitting. We take the time to understand your hearing needs, lifestyle, and budget. We also provide follow-up care and adjustments, which are very important when getting used to hearing aids.

Is Costco a Good Place to Get a Hearing Aid?

Costco is a good starting point for learning about hearing aids. Their prices are lower than many clinics, and they carry strong brands. But there are some limits.

Most Costco locations do not have licensed audiologists. Instead, they use hearing aid specialists. These specialists are trained to help with hearing aids, but they do not have the same medical background or education as an audiologist. If you have complex hearing loss, tinnitus, or other ear problems, a specialist may miss something important.

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, you work with experienced audiologists who understand both the medical and personal sides of hearing loss. We also perform real-ear measurements, which help us fit your hearing aids just right. This step is not always done at big retail stores like Costco.

So while Costco may be cheaper upfront, the personalized care you get at a hearing center can make a big difference in how well you hear every day.

Costco In-Store Services vs. Our Hearing Center

Costco offers in-store services such as:

  • Basic hearing tests
  • Hearing aid fitting
  • Follow-up cleanings and adjustments
  • Free hearing aid demos

But these services are often limited by time and equipment. Their hearing tests are screening-level tests, not full evaluations. And not every store offers real-ear measurements, which are very important for a proper fit.

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, our services include:

  • Full hearing evaluations
  • Real-ear measurements
  • Tinnitus management
  • Custom ear protection
  • Ongoing support and fine-tuning
  • Repairs, batteries, and accessories

We take the time to get to know you. Your hearing needs are personal—and so is our care. Our team is here to help you at every step, not just at the time of purchase.

Things to Consider When Purchasing Costco Hearing Aids

If you’re thinking about buying hearing aids from Costco, here are some important things to keep in mind:

Price

Costco hearing aids are more affordable upfront. But they may not include services like detailed hearing tests or long-term adjustments. At our center, your purchase includes expert care and ongoing support.

Warranty

Costco offers warranties, but they may vary depending on the brand. At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we explain all warranty options and help you with any needed repairs.

Battery

Some Costco hearing aids use disposable batteries, while others are rechargeable. We offer both options and help you decide what works best for your lifestyle.

Accessories

Costco may offer limited accessories. At our hearing center, we carry a wide range of products to make your hearing experience even better—like remote microphones, TV streamers, and custom molds.

Smartphone App & Connectivity

Most Costco hearing aids come with smartphone apps. We also offer high-tech hearing aids with easy app controls and Bluetooth features. Our team will help you set them up and teach you how to use them.

Does Costco Have Audiologists?

In most cases, no. Costco hearing centers are staffed by hearing aid specialists, not licensed audiologists. While specialists can fit and adjust hearing aids, they don’t have the same medical training. That means they may miss signs of other hearing problems, like ear infections or balance disorders.

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, our audiologists are trained to test your hearing, find the cause of your hearing loss, and fit hearing aids that match your needs. We also provide care for things like tinnitus and ear wax buildup.

Choose care from the experts. Our audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL will ensure that your hearing health is in good hands. 

Our clinics are located in Tampa Palms, FL, Tampa Westchase, FL, Palm Harbor, FL, Venice, FL, Sarasota, FL, Bonita Springs, FL, and Alpharetta, GA.

data.meta_title: Crackling in the Ear | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Hearing crackling or popping in your ear? Learn what might be causing it and why it’s important to see an audiologist.
data.content:
Have you ever heard a crackling, popping, or crunching sound in your ear that no one else can hear? It may sound like crinkling paper or the pop of a fizzy drink. For many people, this strange sensation comes and goes, but for others, it can become a constant or concerning experience. 

If you’ve been wondering why your ear crackles and whether it’s something to worry about, you’re not alone.

At Audiology and Hearing Centers, we help patients every day understand and treat unusual ear sounds, including crackling. Contact us today to be connected to expert audiologists in Alpharetta, GA, Tampa Bay, and Venice, FL.

Let’s take a closer look at what might be causing it and how an audiologist can help.

What Causes Crackling in the Ear?

Crackling in the ear can come from several different causes. While many are not serious, some may need medical treatment or professional hearing care. Here are a few of the most common reasons:

1. Eustachian Tube Dysfunction

Your eustachian tubes connect your middle ear to the back of your nose and throat. These tiny passageways help equalize pressure in your ears. If they become blocked due to allergies, sinus infections, colds, or changes in altitude, you might hear popping or crackling sounds. This is one of the most common causes of ear crackling.

2. Ear Wax Buildup

When ear wax gets impacted or is too close to the eardrum, it can cause all sorts of odd noises, including crackling. Wax can trap air and moisture, making the ear feel clogged or cause sounds when you move your jaw. Audiology and Hearing Centers provide professional ear wax removal in Palm Harbor, FL and other key locations.

3. Middle Ear Infections

Infections in the middle ear often lead to a fluid buildup behind the eardrum. As this fluid shifts, it may create a crackling or bubbling sound. These infections are especially common in children but can affect people of all ages.

4. Temporomandibular Joint (TMJ) Disorders

The TMJ is the joint that connects your jaw to your skull. It’s located close to your ear, so issues with this joint can cause sensations or noises in the ear, including popping or crackling when you chew or speak.

5. Changes in Ear Pressure

Changes in elevation (like flying or driving through mountains) can affect the pressure in your ears. As your ears adjust, you may hear a crackling or popping sound. This usually resolves on its own.

When Should You See an Audiologist?

While occasional crackling might not be a big concern, persistent or uncomfortable symptoms should be checked out. You should schedule an appointment with an audiologist if you experience:

  • Ongoing crackling or popping noises
  • A feeling of fullness or pressure in your ear
  • Hearing loss or muffled sounds
  • Dizziness or balance problems
  • Ear pain or fluid drainage

An audiologist can examine your ears, assess your hearing, and determine if the problem is related to earwax, pressure, or something more serious. If needed, they can also refer you to a physician or ENT specialist for further care.

Audiology and Hearing Center connects you with trusted audiologists providing hearing tests in Tampa Palms, FL, Sarasota, Venice, and nearby Tampa Bay communities.

How Audiology and Hearing Center Can Help

At Audiology and Hearing Center, we specialize in diagnosing and treating hearing and ear-related concerns in patients of all ages. Our experienced team uses advanced equipment to look inside your ear canal, test your hearing, and evaluate any blockages or pressure problems. Depending on the cause, we can help:

  • Remove wax safely and comfortably through professional ear wax removal
  • Treat or manage eustachian tube dysfunction
  • Recommend sound therapy or hearing solutions if hearing loss is present
  • Refer you to the right medical provider if needed

With audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL areas, we offer personalized and compassionate care. You don’t have to live with uncomfortable ear symptoms or guess what’s going on, we’re here to give you answers and relief.

Don’t Ignore That Crackling Sound

While ear crackling is usually not a medical emergency, it can be a sign that something’s not working quite right. If it’s bothering you or affecting your hearing, it’s worth getting checked out. Early treatment can often prevent larger problems down the road.

Types of Hearing Protection

Ready-Made Hearing Protection

Pre-formed and mass-produced, ready-made protection is affordable and convenient. However, it may not offer the best fit or comfort for long-term use.

Earplugs

Sleep Plugs

In-Ear Monitors

Popular among musicians and performers. Pros: high sound fidelity. Cons: more expensive than standard plugs.

SoundGear

Hearing Protection for Music

Hearing Protection for Swimming

Industrial Hearing Protection

Types of Custom Ear Molds

data.content:
Audiology & Hearing Centers has audiologists providing diagnostic services in Tampa Palms, Venice, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Sarasota, Bonita Springs, Naples, Ocala FL and Alpharetta, GA.

Diagnostic hearing tests are specialized evaluations that assess your hearing ability and detect any underlying conditions affecting your ears. These tests help determine the type, severity, and cause of hearing loss, allowing audiologists to recommend the best treatment options, whether it’s hearing aids, medical intervention, or other hearing solutions.

We offer diagnostic services for the following:

Tinnitus

Tinnitus is a ringing, buzzing, hissing, clicking, or musical sound in the ears that doesn’t come from an outside source. If you experience persistent tinnitus, a tinnitus evaluation can help identify possible causes and treatment options.

During a comprehensive hearing evaluation, an audiologist will ask about your symptoms, perform a hearing test, and use specialized tests to assess how tinnitus affects your hearing and daily life. Based on the results, treatment options such as sound therapy, hearing aids, or relaxation techniques may be recommended.

If you’re struggling with tinnitus, schedule a tinnitus evaluation today to find relief and improve your quality of life.

Hearing Loss

Hearing loss happens when you have trouble hearing sounds clearly. It can be mild, moderate, severe, or profound and may affect one or both ears. Common symptoms include:

  • Difficulty understanding speech, especially in noisy places
  • Frequently asking people to repeat themselves
  • Turning up the volume on the TV or phone
  • Ringing or buzzing in the ears (tinnitus)

A hearing evaluation with an audiologist is the best way to diagnose hearing loss. The process includes:

  • A medical history review to understand symptoms and risk factors
  • A physical ear exam to check for blockages or infections
  • Hearing tests to measure how well you hear sounds and speech in quiet and in background noise

Sensorineural Hearing Loss

Sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) happens when there is damage to the inner ear (cochlea) or the hearing nerve (auditory nerve). This type of hearing loss is permanent and affects how the brain processes sound.

Symptoms of Sensorineural Hearing Loss

  • Muffled or distorted speech
  • Trouble understanding conversations, especially in noisy places
  • Difficulty hearing high-pitched sounds (birds, doorbells, alarms)
  • Ringing or buzzing in the ears (tinnitus)

Sudden Sensorineural Hearing Loss

Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL), or sudden deafness, is a rapid loss of hearing that happens within hours or days, usually in one ear. It is considered a medical emergency and and should be treated as soon as possible with medication by calling Urgent Care or your physician.

Symptoms of SSNHL

  • Sudden hearing loss in one ear
  • A feeling of fullness or pressure in the ear
  • Dizziness or balance problems
  • Ringing in the ear (tinnitus)

An Audiologist will perform a hearing examination to confirm sudden hearing loss and may refer you to a doctor for medical intervention.

data.meta_title: Rechargeable Hearing Aids | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Tired of changing tiny hearing aid batteries? Discover the comfort and convenience of rechargeable hearing aids at Audiology and Hearing Center.
data.content:
Hearing aids have come a long way in recent years, and one of the most exciting advancements is the development of rechargeable hearing aids. These devices offer a modern solution for individuals with hearing loss, eliminating the need for traditional disposable batteries.

Rechargeable hearing aids are becoming increasingly popular because they’re easy to use, environmentally friendly, and provide all-day power with simple overnight charging. For people who want a low-maintenance, dependable hearing solution, rechargeable hearing aids offer unmatched convenience.

How Rechargeable Hearing Aids Work

Rechargeable hearing aids are powered by built-in lithium-ion batteries—the same reliable battery technology used in smartphones and other modern electronics. Unlike traditional hearing aids that require users to purchase and change small button batteries, rechargeable devices use charging stations or docks to restore battery life.

Charging is simple. Just place your hearing aids in their charger before bedtime, and they’ll be ready to go by morning. Some models even offer quick-charge features, giving you several hours of use from just a short charging session. With no battery doors to open and close, they’re a great fit for people with limited hand strength or vision.

Benefits of Rechargeable Hearing Aids

1. Convenience
One of the biggest advantages of rechargeable hearing aids is how easy they are to use. There’s no need to fumble with tiny batteries or worry about your hearing aids shutting down during the day because of a dead battery. A full night of charging gives you a full day of hearing.

2. Environmentally Friendly
Disposable hearing aid batteries add to environmental waste. Rechargeable hearing aids reduce the need for single-use batteries, making them a greener, more sustainable option.

3. Cost-Effective
Over time, replacing disposable batteries can become expensive. Rechargeable hearing aids may have a higher upfront cost, but they save money in the long run by eliminating ongoing battery purchases.

4. Long Battery Life
Depending on usage, modern rechargeable models can run up to 24-30 hours on a single charge. This means less stress about your hearing aids running out of power during the day.

5. Easy Charging
With most devices offering straightforward charging stations, all you need to do is plug them in at night. Many chargers even double as protective cases—perfect for travel or storage.

Rechargeable Hearing Aids vs. Traditional Battery Hearing Aids

Rechargeable Hearing Aids:

✔ No need to buy or replace batteries
✔ Environmentally friendly
✔ Easier to handle for people with dexterity or vision challenges
✔ Consistent daily performance
✘ Slightly higher upfront cost

Traditional Battery Hearing Aids:

✔ Lower initial price
✔ Batteries are available at most stores
✘ Need to replace batteries every few days
✘ Small batteries can be hard to manage
✘ More waste is produced over time

For many people—especially those who value simplicity or have trouble with fine motor tasks—rechargeable hearing aids are the clear winner.

Who Can Benefit From Rechargeable Hearing Aids?

Rechargeable hearing aids are a smart choice for nearly anyone with hearing loss, whether mild, moderate, or profound. But they’re especially helpful for:

  • People looking for a low-maintenance option – Just charge overnight and go.
  • Those with arthritis or hand weakness – No tiny batteries to manage.
  • Anyone with visual impairments – Easier handling, no small parts.
  • Busy individuals – Fewer interruptions and less upkeep.

If you’re someone who values simplicity, sustainability, and modern technology, rechargeable hearing aids may be right for you.

FAQs About Rechargeable Hearing Aids

How long does the battery last?

Most rechargeable hearing aids last 24 to 30 hours on a full charge, depending on usage and features like Bluetooth streaming.

How often do I need to charge my hearing aids?

Daily charging is recommended. Plug them in at night, and you’ll wake up with fully charged hearing aids.

What if I forget to charge them?

Some models offer quick-charge options—just 15–30 minutes in the charger can give you several hours of use.

Are rechargeable hearing aids water-resistant?

Yes, most models are water-resistant, making them suitable for everyday activities like exercising or being outdoors in humid weather. However, they are not waterproof.

What happens when the battery eventually wears out?

Built-in batteries are designed to last several years. When the battery’s performance declines, your audiologist can replace it or help you upgrade your device.

Rechargeable Hearing Aids in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL

Our team will help you choose the best rechargeable hearing aid for your lifestyle and hearing level.

Whether you’re new to hearing aids or ready for an upgrade, we’re here to make the process smooth and stress-free.

Schedule Your Consultation Today

Ready to experience the freedom of rechargeable hearing aids? Contact Audiology and Hearing Center in Tampa Bay or Venice to book a hearing evaluation. Let our experienced audiologists help you find the perfect fit for your hearing needs—and your life.

data.meta_title: Do They Really Work? | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Learn the truth about ear candles, their risks, and safer ways to clean ear wax. Discover expert advice and why seeing an audiologist is the best option.
data.content:
You’ve probably seen ear candles at wellness shops or online and wondered if they’re a good way to remove ear wax. They claim to “draw out” wax and impurities from your ears using heat and suction, but are they really safe or effective? 

Ear wax is a natural part of ear health, and trying to remove it improperly can cause more harm than good. Before trying something risky like ear candling, it’s important to learn the facts and know when to visit an audiologist for help with ear cleaning or general ear care.

Overview of Ear Candling

Ear candling involves placing a hollow candle into the ear canal and lighting the exposed end. As the candle burns, it supposedly creates suction to pull out wax and debris. However, there’s no scientific proof this actually happens. Instead, melted wax from the candle itself often ends up inside the ear. 

Ear wax plays an important role in keeping the ears clean and protecting them from dust, germs, and water. Because of these risks, audiologists recommend avoiding ear candles and turning to safer, medically approved methods.

How Are Ear Candles Used?

An ear candle is typically about 10 inches long and made of cloth soaked in wax. The person lies on their side while another person inserts the tapered end of the candle into the ear. The wider end is lit, and the candle is allowed to burn for several minutes. Some people believe the heat draws wax out, but what’s often found in the candle after use is just melted candle wax, not ear wax. This method is not only ineffective, but it can also be dangerous.

What Do Experts Say About Ear Candling?

Experts strongly advise against using ear candles. Audiologists and medical professionals agree that ear candling doesn’t actually remove wax and can even create new problems. Burns, ear canal injuries, and wax blockages from candle residue are just a few of the common issues people report. 

Studies have shown that the “wax” seen after ear candling is not from the ear, it’s from the candle itself. Health professionals emphasize that the risk of injury outweighs any perceived benefits. Simply put, there are much safer and more effective ways to care for your ears.

What Does the FDA Say About Ear Candles?

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has issued multiple warnings about the dangers of ear candles. The FDA classifies ear candles as unapproved medical devices and prohibits their sale for earwax removal. They warn that using these products can lead to serious injuries, including burns, punctured eardrums, and hearing loss. 

The FDA has taken action against companies that market ear candles with false claims, stating that they offer no proven benefits and pose unnecessary health risks.

How Can I Remove Wax Safely?

Ear wax is a healthy part of ear function and doesn’t usually need to be removed unless it causes a blockage. If you feel pressure, hear muffled sounds, or have discomfort, it’s best to see an audiologist. Over-the-counter drops can help soften wax so it can exit the ear naturally. In some cases, a professional might use gentle irrigation or specialized tools to remove it safely. 

Ear candles put you at risk for burns, blockage, and damage. It’s just not worth the risk.

What Are Safer Methods to Clean Our Ears?

Cleaning your ears doesn’t have to be complicated or dangerous. Here are safe ways to manage earwax:

  • Use a warm, damp washcloth to gently clean the outer ear.
  • Avoid inserting objects, like cotton swabs, into the ear canal.
  • Try ear drops made to soften wax (ask a pharmacist or doctor).
  • Visit an audiologist if you have frequent wax buildup or discomfort. These methods protect your ear canal from injury and help maintain healthy hearing. Your ears are self-cleaning, and most people don’t need to do much at all. If you suspect a problem, always turn to a professional instead of risky DIY methods like ear candles.

Audiology and Hearing Centers has audiologists providing professional ear wax removal in Alpharetta, GA, Tampa Bay, FL, Venice, FL, and nearby areas. We are here to help with any hearing concerns and take care of your hearing health.

Professional Ear Wax Removal in Tampa Bay, FL and Venice, FL

Ear candling may seem like a natural remedy, but it’s not a safe or effective method for removing earwax. Medical experts and the FDA agree, ear candles can do more harm than good. They can lead to burns, blockages, and even hearing loss. 

Ear wax has a purpose, and the safest way to deal with buildup is through gentle cleaning or a visit to an audiologist. 

Don’t take chances with your hearing. Schedule a professional ear check-up and ear wax removal at Audiology and Hearing Centers today!

data.meta_description: Audiology & Hearing Center has audiologists providing professional ear wax removal in Tampa Westchase, FL.
data.content:
Audiology & Hearing Centers has audiologists providing professional ear wax removal in Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Sarasota, Bonita Springs FL and Alpharetta, GA.

Ear wax is a natural substance that helps keep your ears clean and protected. However, too much ear wax can cause problems, like muffled hearing, discomfort, or even pain. 

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we offer safe and professional ear wax removal to help you hear clearly and feel comfortable again.

What Is Ear Wax?

Ear wax, also called cerumen, is made by tiny glands in your ear canal. It traps dirt, dust, and bacteria to keep your ears clean. Most of the time, ear wax moves out of the ear on its own. But sometimes, it builds up and causes blockages.

Signs You May Have Too Much Ear Wax

If you have too much ear wax, you might notice:

  • Muffled hearing – Sounds may seem blocked or distant.
  • Ear pain or pressure – Too much wax can make your ears feel full.
  • Ringing in the ears (tinnitus) – You may hear buzzing or ringing.
  • Itching or discomfort – Your ears may feel irritated.
  • Dizziness – A wax buildup can sometimes cause balance problems.
  • Coughing – Blocked ears can affect the nerves connected to your throat.

If you have any of these symptoms, a professional ear wax removal can help.

Why You Shouldn’t Remove Ear Wax at Home

Many people try to clean their ears at home using cotton swabs, ear candles, or other tools. However, these methods can push the wax deeper, making the blockage worse. Some home treatments can even damage your ear canal or eardrum.

Safe ways to manage ear wax at home include:

  • Using ear drops – Special drops can soften the wax so it can come out naturally.
  • Wiping the outer ear – Use a soft, damp cloth to clean around the outside of your ear.
  • Letting your ears clean themselves – Your ears naturally remove wax as you chew and move your jaw.

However, if ear wax becomes stuck or causes discomfort, it’s best to see a professional for safe removal.

Professional Ear Wax Removal Methods

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, our trained audiologists use safe and effective methods to remove ear wax:

1. Irrigation (Ear Flushing)

This method uses warm water to gently flush out ear wax. The audiologist directs a stream of water into the ear canal to wash away the buildup.

2. Microsuction

A small, gentle suction device is used to remove ear wax. This method is quick and comfortable and is often recommended for people with sensitive ears or ear infections.

3. Manual Removal

The audiologist may use special tools like a curette to carefully remove ear wax by hand. This is done with great care to avoid injury.

The best method for your ears will depend on how much wax is present and your ear health.

How Often Should You Have Ear Wax Removed?

Most people don’t need regular ear wax removal because the ears clean themselves. However, some people produce more ear wax than others and may need professional cleaning every few months.

You may need more frequent ear wax removal if you:

  • Wear hearing aids or earbuds often.
  • Have narrow or curvy ear canals.
  • Have a history of ear infections.
  • Have dry or flaky skin conditions like eczema.

Your audiologist can help determine the best schedule for you.

Can Ear Wax Affect Hearing Aids?

Yes! Too much ear wax can block the tiny openings of a hearing aid, making it work poorly or stop working. If you wear hearing aids, it’s important to keep your ears clean to maintain clear sound and prevent damage to the devices.

Why Choose Audiology & Hearing Center for Ear Wax Removal?

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we provide:


✔ Safe and gentle ear wax removal methods.
✔ Expert care from experienced audiologists.
✔ Personalized treatment based on your ear health.
✔ Comfortable and quick appointments to fit your schedule.

We care about your hearing and want to help you feel your best.

Schedule Your Ear Wax Removal Today!

If you’re experiencing ear blockage, muffled hearing, or discomfort, we’re here to help.

Contact us today to schedule an appointment!

data.meta_title: Assistive Listening Devices | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Explore hearing aid alternatives at Audiology & Hearing Center in Tampa Bay & Venice, FL. Learn how assistive listening devices (ALDs) can improve hearing in everyday situations.
data.content:
If hearing aids aren’t the right fit for you, assistive listening devices (ALDs) may help. These tools make sounds louder and clearer in places where background noise or distance makes hearing difficult. Whether you’re watching TV, talking on the phone, or attending a group meeting, ALDs can improve your ability to hear. 

At Audiology & Hearing Centers in Tampa Palms, Venice, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Sarasota FL and Alpharetta, GA, we offer several types of ALDs to help you stay connected to the sounds that matter most.

Assistive Listening Devices vs. Hearing Aids

Hearing aids amplify sound and are custom-fit for your hearing loss. They help in many everyday situations, but sometimes you need extra help in specific environments. That’s where assistive listening devices come in.

ALDs are designed to boost sound in challenging settings—like classrooms, theaters, or while watching TV. They can work alongside hearing aids or be used alone for people with mild hearing loss. Both options help you hear better, but ALDs are usually task-specific.

Unlike hearing aids, ALDs don’t require a hearing test or custom fitting. They are often more affordable and easier to use, making them a good alternative or complement to hearing aids.

Types of Assistive Listening Devices

Audio Induction or Hearing Loop Systems

Description:
 Hearing loops use electromagnetic fields to deliver sound directly to hearing aids or cochlear implants with a telecoil (T-coil).

Features/Benefits:

  • Clear sound with less background noise
  • Works well in public spaces like theaters and churches
  • Wireless connection to T-coil-equipped hearing aids

Infrared Systems

Description:
 Infrared ALDs use invisible light waves to carry sound to a receiver, which you wear via headphones or neck loops.

Features/Benefits:

  • Great for private communication
  • Works well for TV listening or in courtrooms
  • No radio interference

FM Systems

Description:
 FM systems use radio waves to send sound from a microphone (worn by the speaker) to a receiver (used by the listener).

Features/Benefits:

  • Great for classrooms and group settings
  • Reduces distance and background noise issues
  • Compatible with hearing aids and cochlear implants

Personal Amplified Systems

Description:
 Personal amplifiers boost sound close to you. These handheld or pocket-sized devices come with headphones or earbuds.

Features/Benefits:

  • Simple and affordable
  • Great for one-on-one conversations or TV listening
  • No professional fitting required

TV Listening Systems

Description:
 These devices transmit TV audio to wireless headphones or hearing aid-compatible receivers.

Features/Benefits:

  • Direct sound without turning up the volume for others
  • Crisp, clear dialogue
  • Multiple connection options (Bluetooth, infrared, RF)

Alerting Devices

Description:
 These devices use flashing lights, loud sounds, or vibrations to alert you to sounds like doorbells, alarms, or phone calls.

Features/Benefits:

  • Provides safety and awareness
  • Works well for people with severe hearing loss
  • Can be connected to smartphones and home systems

Assistive Listening Devices FAQs

What is the most commonly used assistive listening device?

The most common ALD is the FM system. It’s popular in schools, lecture halls, and group settings. Teachers or speakers wear a microphone, and sound is sent directly to the listener’s receiver. This helps people hear better in noisy environments or at a distance. FM systems are reliable, portable, and work well with hearing aids or cochlear implants equipped with T-coils or audio inputs.

Why are assistive listening devices necessary?

ALDs are necessary because hearing aids alone may not be enough in certain environments. Background noise, echo, and distance can make hearing difficult even with hearing aids. ALDs give extra help by improving sound quality in specific situations like public events, classrooms, or watching TV. They enhance communication and safety, helping people stay active and independent in everyday life.

Can assistive listening devices take the place of hearing aids?

ALDs can help in specific situations, but they don’t fully replace hearing aids for those with moderate to severe hearing loss. Hearing aids are worn all day and offer continuous support, while ALDs are used only in certain settings. Some people with mild hearing loss may prefer using ALDs instead of hearing aids. However, for everyday hearing needs, most people benefit from using both together.

Assistive Listening Devices in Tampa Bay & Venice, FL

Assistive listening devices are great hearing aid alternatives that can help you hear more clearly in certain settings. Whether you’re watching TV, attending church, or talking to loved ones, ALDs make sound easier to hear and understand.

At Audiology & Hearing Centers in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL, we can help you choose the right device for your needs. 

Our clinics are located in Tampa Palms, FL, Tampa Westchase, FL, Palm Harbor, FL, Venice, FL, Sarasota, FL, and Alpharetta, GA. 

Call us today to schedule a consultation and explore your options for better hearing—no matter where life takes you.

data.content:
Audiology & Hearing Centers provides hearing aid batteries in Tampa Palms, Venice, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Sarasota, Bonita Springs FL and Alpharetta, GA, to ensure that your hearing devices are always functioning at their best.

Hearing aid batteries power your hearing aids, making them work properly. Without the right battery, your hearing aids won’t help you hear well. Batteries affect how long your hearing aids last each day and how well they perform. Choosing the right type of battery is important for convenience and cost.

Two Types of Hearing Aid Batteries

Hearing aids use two main types of batteries: rechargeable batteries and standard disposable batteries. Each type has its own benefits.

Rechargeable Battery

Rechargeable batteries are built into the hearing aid and do not need to be replaced often. You simply place your hearing aids in a charging case or dock overnight. By morning, they are fully charged and ready to use.

Hearing aid batteries power your hearing aids, making them work properly. Without the right battery, your hearing aids won’t help you hear well. Batteries affect how long your hearing aids last each day and how well they perform. Choosing the right type of battery is important for convenience and cost.

Benefits of rechargeable batteries:

  • No need to buy new batteries often
  • Easy to charge overnight
  • Better for the environment since they don’t create waste
  • Ideal for people who have trouble handling small batteries

However, rechargeable hearing aids must be charged daily. If you forget to charge them, they won’t work until recharged.

Standard Disposable Batteries

Disposable batteries are small, round batteries that you replace when they run out. These are also called zinc-air batteries. They are activated when you remove a small sticker from the battery, allowing air to enter.

Benefits of disposable batteries:

  • Can be replaced quickly when they run out
  • Useful if you don’t have a power source nearby to recharge
  • Last different lengths of time, depending on the battery size and hearing aid type

Disposable batteries can be helpful for people who travel often or do not have access to a charger. However, they need to be replaced regularly, which means ongoing costs.

Sizes of Disposable Hearing Aid Batteries

Disposable hearing aid batteries come in different sizes. Each size fits specific hearing aids.

  • Size 10 – Smallest battery, used in very small hearing aids. Lasts about 3-5 days.
  • Size 312 – A little bigger, common in behind-the-ear (BTE) and in-the-ear (ITE) hearing aids. Lasts about 5-7 days.
  • Size 13 – Larger than 312, used in powerful hearing aids. Lasts about 6-14 days.
  • Size 675 – Largest battery, used in very strong hearing aids. Lasts about 9-20 days.

Color-Coding for Disposable Hearing Aid Batteries

To make it easy to find the right size, battery packages use color codes:

  • Size 10 – Yellow
  • Size 312 – Brown
  • Size 13 – Orange
  • Size 675 – Blue

Battery Life of Hearing Aid Batteries

How long a hearing aid battery lasts depends on the battery size, the type of hearing aid, and how often you use it. On average:

  • Small batteries last 3-7 days
  • Larger batteries last 6-20 days
  • Rechargeable batteries last a full day but need daily charging

Using Bluetooth and other advanced hearing aid features can drain batteries faster.

How to Extend the Life of a Hearing Aid Battery

Taking care of your hearing aid batteries can help them last longer. Here are some tips:

Proper Storage and Handling:

  • Keep batteries in a cool, dry place.
  • Do not store them in the refrigerator.
  • Keep them away from metal objects, like keys and coins, to avoid short-circuiting.

Activating Zinc-Air Batteries:

  • Remove the sticker only when you are ready to use the battery.
  • Let the battery sit for 1-2 minutes before placing it in the hearing aid. This allows air to activate the battery fully.

Extending the Life of Rechargeable Batteries:

  • Charge hearing aids every night.
  • Do not overcharge them—use the charger designed for your hearing aid.
  • If storing hearing aids for a long time, turn them off to save battery life.

When to Replace Batteries:

  • If sounds become weak or unclear. If your hearing still hasn’t improved even after battery replacement, you may need to contact your hearing aid provider so they can check your device. If the device is okay, you may need to get a hearing test or ear wax removal (if the check-up shows an ear wax blockage that could be affecting how you hear).
  • If your hearing aid does not turn on
  • If you hear beeping sounds from the hearing aid (a low battery warning)

Visit Audiology & Hearing Centers today or contact us to find the right batteries for your hearing aids!

A hearing aid fitting is the process of setting up and adjusting hearing aids to match your specific hearing needs. This step is important to ensure you hear clearly and comfortably. Hearing aids are not just about making sounds louder—they need to be fine-tuned to match your unique level of hearing loss.

Why is a Professional Hearing Aid Fitting Important?

Getting a professional hearing aid fitting is essential for the best hearing experience. An audiologist understands how to program your hearing aids so they work properly for your hearing loss. A good fitting helps reduce background noise, improve speech clarity, and make everyday sounds feel natural.

Why Should an Audiologist Fit Your Hearing Aids?

An audiologist is trained to adjust hearing aids based on your specific hearing needs. They use special tools and tests to make sure your hearing aids are working correctly. A poorly fitted hearing aid can cause discomfort, feedback noise, or ineffective sound amplification. That’s why seeing an audiologist is the best way to ensure a proper fit.

What Happens During a Hearing Aid Fitting?

Step-by-Step Process of a Hearing Aid Fitting

  1. Reviewing Your Hearing Test Results – The audiologist will go over your hearing test results to understand your hearing loss.
  2. Selecting the Right Hearing Aid – Based on your needs and lifestyle, the audiologist will recommend a hearing aid style and technology.
  3. Programming the Hearing Aids – The hearing aids are adjusted using a computer to match your specific hearing loss.
  4. Real Ear Measurement – A small microphone is placed in your ear to measure how the hearing aids are amplifying sounds.
  5. Learning How to Use Your Hearing Aids – The audiologist will show you how to insert, remove, and care for your hearing aids.
  6. Scheduling Follow-up Appointments – Your hearing aids may need fine-tuning after you wear them for a few days.

What to Expect

Your first hearing aid fitting is an exciting step toward better hearing. You will notice new sounds, and it may take some time to adjust. The audiologist will answer your questions and make sure the hearing aids feel comfortable in your ears.

Real Ear Measurement

What is Real Ear Measurement?

Real Ear Measurement (REM) is a test that ensures your hearing aids are providing the correct amount of amplification. A small microphone is placed in your ear while you wear your hearing aids. This measures how well the hearing aids are delivering sound.

Why is Real Ear Measurement Important?

Every ear is different, and REM helps audiologists adjust your hearing aids for the best results. Without REM, the hearing aid settings might not be accurate. This test ensures your hearing aids work properly for your specific hearing loss.

Adjusting to Hearing Aids

What to Expect

When you first wear hearing aids, your brain needs time to relearn sounds. Everyday noises, like the rustling of leaves or the hum of the refrigerator, may seem loud at first. Over time, your brain will adjust, and these sounds will feel more natural.

The Adjustment Period

Most people need a few weeks to fully adjust to their hearing aids. Here are some tips to help:

  • Start by wearing your hearing aids for a few hours a day.
  • Gradually increase wear time as you get comfortable.
  • Practice listening in quiet places before moving to noisy settings.
  • Be patient and give your brain time to adapt.

Follow-up Appointments

Follow-up appointments are crucial for ensuring your hearing aids are working well. Your audiologist will make adjustments based on your feedback. If the hearing aids feel uncomfortable or if sounds are too loud or too soft, these can be corrected during follow-ups.

Hearing Aid Fitting FAQs

Are hearing aid fittings covered by insurance?

Some insurance plans cover hearing aid fittings, but coverage varies. Medicare does not typically cover hearing aids, but some private insurance and Veterans Affairs benefits may help. Check with your provider to see what is included in your plan.

How can i make my hearing aid fit better?

If your hearing aid feels uncomfortable, your audiologist can adjust the fit. There are different ear tips and custom molds available to improve comfort. Never try to force a hearing aid into your ear—this can cause pain and reduce effectiveness.

How long does it take for a hearing aid to be fitted?

The initial fitting usually takes about an hour. However, it can take a few weeks of adjustments to get the best fit and sound quality. Regular follow-ups help ensure your hearing aids are working optimally.

Hearing Aid Styles

Hearing aids come in different styles, each designed for different needs:

  • In-the-canal (IIC) – smallest and invisible, best for mild to moderate hearing loss. Pros: More features than CIC. Cons: Still small, may be hard to adjust.
  • Completely-in-the-Canal (CIC) – Small and nearly invisible, best for mild to moderate hearing loss. Pros: Discreet. Cons: Shorter battery life, can be difficult to handle.
  • In-the-Ear (ITE) – Fits in the outer ear, easier to handle. Pros: Larger battery, easier controls. Cons: More visible than ITC or CIC.
  • Behind-the-Ear (BTE) – Rests behind the ear with a tube leading to an earpiece. Pros: Suitable for all levels of hearing loss, easy to adjust. Cons: More noticeable.

Once you select a hearing aid, our audiologists ensure a comfortable and secure fit. We take ear impressions and customize the device for your ear shape and hearing needs. A proper fit ensures clear sound and prevents discomfort.

Hearing Aid Programming and Adjustments

Counseling and Education
