Hearing Aid Brands/Manufacturers

Audiology & Hearing Centers provides and services most major hearing aid brands. Whether you already wear hearing aids or are looking for a new device, our audiologists can help with programming, adjustments, cleaning, and repairs. 

We work with a wide range of trusted brands to make sure you get the best hearing care possible.

Phonak Hearing Aids

Phonak is one of the most trusted hearing aid brands in the world. The company has been helping people hear better for over 75 years. Phonak is known for making smart, easy-to-use hearing aids with helpful features to make hearing easier for people of all ages. Their popular models include Phonak Lumity, which offers clear speech understanding in noise, and Phonak Naída, a strong option for people with more severe hearing loss. Many Phonak models include Bluetooth, rechargeable batteries, and water-resistant designs.

Oticon Hearing Aids

Oticon has been helping people hear better since 1904. Their hearing aids use BrainHearing™ technology, which helps your brain understand sound in a natural way. Oticon hearing aids are designed to reduce listening effort and improve sound clarity. Popular models include Oticon Real and Oticon More, which help wearers hear speech clearly even in noisy environments. These hearing aids also offer tinnitus relief and wireless connectivity.

Resound Hearing Aids

ReSound has been making hearing aids for more than 75 years. They are known for their natural sound quality and strong Bluetooth performance. Models like ReSound Omnia and ReSound ONE offer personalized hearing with features like sound direction and speech clarity in noise. ReSound hearing aids work well with smartphones and also offer rechargeable options and tinnitus management tools.

Unitron Hearing Aids

Unitron has been creating hearing aids for decades, focusing on comfort and simplicity. Their hearing aids adjust automatically to different sound environments, helping you hear better in everyday situations. Popular models like Unitron Blu offer Bluetooth streaming, rechargeable batteries, and sleek designs. Unitron hearing aids are also known for easy adjustments through mobile apps and flexible upgrade options.

Widex Hearing Aids

Widex has been making hearing aids since 1956 and is known for rich, natural sound quality. Their Widex Moment and Widex SmartRIC models reduce sound delay, making speech and noise sound more real and clear. Widex hearing aids are especially popular for people with tinnitus, offering built-in sound therapy programs. Many models also connect to smartphones and offer rechargeable options.

Starkey Hearing Aids

Starkey is an American brand known for combining hearing technology with overall health features. Their hearing aids, such as Starkey Genesis AI and Evolv AI, offer features like fall detection, activity tracking, and tinnitus relief. They work well with smartphones and offer hands-free calling, rechargeable batteries, and health monitoring tools.

Signia Hearing Aids

Signia hearing aids offer advanced sound technology and stylish designs. Models like Signia Pure Charge&Go and Silk X include rechargeable batteries, Bluetooth connectivity, and clear speech in noisy settings. Signia also offers discreet models that sit deep in the ear and are nearly invisible. Some models include features like own-voice processing for a more natural sound.

Lyric Hearing Aids

Lyric is a special type of hearing aid made by Phonak. It is placed deep in the ear canal by a hearing professional and stays there for months at a time. Lyric is invisible, requires no batteries, and allows natural sound quality. It’s a great option for people who want a completely hidden hearing aid and don’t want to worry about daily maintenance.

CaptionCall

CaptionCall is a free phone service for people with hearing loss who need help understanding conversations. The phone displays real-time captions on a screen as the caller speaks. This makes it easier to follow phone conversations. To qualify, you must have hearing loss and a hearing professional’s certification. Talk to our Tampa Palms, FL audiologist to learn more.

CapTel

CapTel (Captioned Telephone) is another helpful tool for people with hearing loss. It shows written captions of what callers say while you listen and speak as usual. CapTel phones are available in several models and work with a captioning service. It’s a great solution for people who still want to use a regular phone but need a little extra help hearing.

FAQs:

What is the most advanced hearing aid?

Many hearing aids today have advanced features like speech enhancement, Bluetooth, and rechargeable batteries. Different brands offer different benefits. The best hearing aid depends on your hearing needs and lifestyle. Our audiologists can help you choose the right one for you.

Which brand of hearing aid is best for tinnitus?

Several brands offer tinnitus relief features, including Widex, Oticon, and Starkey. But everyone’s needs are different. The best way to find relief is to speak with an audiologist who can recommend the best hearing aid and treatment plan for your tinnitus.

Which hearing aid is best for severe loss?

There are powerful hearing aids designed for people with severe hearing loss. Some examples include models from Phonak, Oticon, and Starkey. But the best option for you depends on your hearing test results. Your audiologist can help guide you to the most effective solution.

Custom Hearing Protection 

Custom earplugs are made to fit your ears exactly. They offer better comfort and hearing protection than store-bought plugs. You can use them for work in loud places, concerts, motorsports, shooting sports, or even swimming. Custom plugs are more secure, last longer, and provide a better seal to protect your hearing without blocking all sound. Our audiologists can help you choose the right type for your lifestyle and make an ear impression for a perfect fit.

Custom Ear Molds

Custom ear molds are made to match the unique shape of your ear. They are most commonly used with custom hearing aids but can also be used for other needs like in-ear monitors, hearing protection, or swim plugs. A hearing care provider takes a quick ear impression, and the mold is made in a lab for a snug and comfortable fit. Custom molds help keep hearing aids in place and reduce sound feedback or whistling. They also improve sound quality and wearing comfort.

Custom Ear Molds for Musicians

Musicians need clear sound and strong protection. Custom ear molds can be used to make musician earplugs or in-ear monitors. These help protect hearing from loud music while keeping the quality of the sound clear. Custom molds also give a secure fit for performances and practice. Whether you’re on stage or in the studio, they help you hear better and stay protected.

FAQs:

Is it OK to sleep with ear plugs in?

Yes, sleeping with earplugs is safe. Just make sure they are clean and fit well to avoid ear irritation or infection.

How long do ear molds last?

Custom ear molds usually last between 2 to 5 years. Their lifespan depends on how often you use them and how well you take care of them.

Why are ear molds used in hearing aid fitting?

Ear molds help hearing aids fit better, reduce sound leaks, and stop feedback (whistling sounds). They also improve comfort and sound quality.

Assistive Listening Devices

Assistive Listening Devices help people hear better in everyday situations like talking on the phone, watching TV, or being in group settings. ALDs can be used alone or along with hearing aids. Common devices include:

  • TV streamers
  • FM systems
  • Personal amplifiers
  • Loop systems
  • Bluetooth microphones

An audiologist can help you choose the best ALD based on your needs. For example, if you struggle in meetings or at church, an FM or loop system may help. If you wear hearing aids, many ALDs can connect directly to them for clearer sound.

These devices can improve your ability to hear clearly, stay involved in conversations, and enjoy everyday activities again.

FAQs:

What is the most commonly used assistive listening device?

TV streamers and personal amplifiers are among the most popular. They help improve hearing in loud or distant places.

Can you turn an iPhone into a listening device?

Yes, Apple’s Live Listen feature lets your iPhone pick up sound and send it to your hearing aids or headphones.

Where are assistive listening devices required?

Places like theaters, churches, classrooms, and government buildings must offer assistive listening systems by law to help people with hearing loss hear better.

data.meta_description: If you have been looking for information on how to cure tinnitus naturally, this guide explains simple steps that may help quiet the ringing and improve your
data.content:
If you have been looking for information on how to cure tinnitus naturally, this guide explains simple steps that may help quiet the ringing and improve your comfort. We explore lifestyle habits, diet changes, relaxation techniques, and sound-based tools that many people find helpful. You’ll also learn what causes tinnitus and when it’s time to seek care from an audiologist at Audiology & Hearing Centers.

Living with tinnitus can feel overwhelming, especially if the ringing, buzzing, or humming never seems to stop. Many people search for how to cure tinnitus naturally, hoping for something gentle and safe to ease the condition. While there is no single cure that makes tinnitus disappear completely, there are many natural methods that can lower symptoms, improve comfort, and help your brain react to tinnitus in a calmer way.

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we support individuals every day who want real answers, not quick fixes. Through natural remedies, healthy habits, sound therapy, and hearing care, most people notice a decrease in how bothersome their tinnitus feels. This article explains the most effective natural options and how they can work together to help you regain balance and peace of mind.

Understanding Why Tinnitus Happens

Tinnitus is not a disease. It is a symptom that means something inside the auditory system is irritated, stressed, or not working as expected. People describe tinnitus as:

  • Ringing
  • Buzzing
  • Hissing
  • Whistling
  • Clicking
  • Pulsing

The tinnitus symptoms or loudness may change day to day. Some hear it only at night, while others hear it all the time.

Common causes include:

  • Hearing loss
  • Exposure to loud noise
  • Stress or anxiety
  • Earwax buildup
  • Certain medications
  • Circulation problems
  • Jaw or neck muscle tension
  • Illness or sinus pressure

Because tinnitus has so many causes, natural relief often depends on choosing the right combination of strategies for your unique situation.

How to Cure Tinnitus Naturally: Helpful Methods That Support Relief

Although tinnitus may not have a complete cure, many people feel better through simple natural habits. These techniques help the body stay healthy, reduce stress, and help the brain pay less attention to the ringing.

Below are the most reliable natural remedies supported by lifestyle science, hearing research, and patient experience.

1. Diet Choices That Support Ear and Brain Health

What you eat can affect your hearing and tinnitus. Eating a balanced diet may help reduce inflammation and support good circulation around the auditory system.

Foods that help:

  • Fresh fruits
  • Leafy greens
  • Nuts and seeds
  • Fish rich in omega-3 fats
  • Whole grains

These foods provide important vitamins and minerals that support nerve, ear, and brain health. Many doctors also recommend reducing:

  • High-sodium foods
  • Caffeine
  • Alcohol
  • Processed snacks

For some people, salty or sugary foods can make tinnitus louder. Keeping a daily food journal can help you learn what worsens or improves your symptoms.

2. Hydration Matters More Than You Think

Staying hydrated is a simple but powerful tool for tinnitus relief. When the body lacks water, blood flow decreases and ear pressure may change, making tinnitus feel louder.

Aim for:

  • Drinking water throughout the day
  • Reducing sugary or caffeinated drinks
  • Choosing water-rich foods (fruit, cucumber, melon)

Hydration supports healthy balance in the inner ear and helps your body function smoothly.

3. Improve Sleep Habits for Calmer Nights

Poor sleep increases stress, and stress often makes tinnitus louder. Good sleep also gives your brain time to rest and adjust to sounds.

Tips for better sleep:

  • Keep a regular bedtime
  • Avoid screens one hour before sleeping
  • Use soft background noise to mask tinnitus
  • Keep your room cool, dark, and quiet

Sound machines or gentle nature noises can bring comfort and help mask ringing at night.

4. Gentle Exercise to Reduce Stress

Exercise helps by improving blood flow, releasing stress, and strengthening your overall health. You do not need intense workouts to feel a difference.

Good choices include:

  • Walking
  • Swimming
  • Stretching
  • Cycling
  • Water aerobics

Exercise also helps the brain release natural chemicals that improve mood and ease anxiety, both important for tinnitus relief.

5. Meditation and Deep Breathing

Meditation helps calm the nervous system and lower the “alarm response” the brain sometimes has to tinnitus. You don’t need long sessions; even 5 minutes can help.

Try:

  • Slow, deep belly breaths
  • Guided relaxation
  • Mindful listening
  • Simple quiet sitting

Over time, meditation helps your brain stay calmer when tinnitus appears. Many patients say the more they practice, the less stressful tinnitus feels.

6. Yoga for Body and Balance

Yoga supports relaxation, flexibility, circulation, and balance. Because tension in the neck and jaw can worsen tinnitus, gentle stretching is often helpful.

Simple yoga poses can:

  • Release tight muscles
  • Reduce pressure around the ears
  • Calm the mind
  • Improve sleep

Chair yoga works well for beginners or anyone with limited mobility.

7. Sound Therapy and Masking Techniques

Sound therapy is a natural method that trains the brain to pay less attention to tinnitus. It does not remove tinnitus, but it makes it easier to live with.

Helpful sound tools include:

  • White noise
  • Soft background music
  • Rain or ocean sounds
  • Fans
  • Tinnitus-masking apps

Hearing aids with sound support features can also help. At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we help many patients find masking sounds that bring comfort. We offer a wide selection of hearing aids in Tampa Bay, Venice, FL, and nearby key locations.

8. Essential Oils for Relaxation

Essential oils do not cure tinnitus, but they may help reduce stress. Lower stress often leads to softer, calmer tinnitus.

Popular calming scents include:

  • Lavender
  • Chamomile
  • Peppermint
  • Rosemary

These can be used in a diffuser or applied lightly to the skin. Never place oils in the ear canal.

9. Vitamins, Minerals, and Herbal Support

Some vitamins, minerals, and herbs may support nervous system health and help reduce tinnitus-related stress.

Common natural options include:

  • Magnesium – supports nerve function
  • Zinc – important for hearing health
  • Vitamin B12 – helps nerve repair
  • Melatonin – supports sleep
  • Ginkgo biloba – may improve circulation

Always talk to a healthcare provider before starting new supplements, especially if you take medication.

10. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)

CBT is one of the most effective long-term treatments for tinnitus. It does not remove the sound, but it teaches the brain to respond differently.

CBT helps by:

  • Reducing stress
  • Lowering fear and worry
  • Improving sleep
  • Building coping skills
  • Reducing emotional discomfort

Many people say CBT completely changes the way they experience tinnitus.

11. Acupuncture for Tension and Stress Relief

Acupuncture may help reduce tinnitus by relaxing tight muscles, improving blood flow, and easing stress. While research is still ongoing, many patients report helpful relief.

How Tinnitus Is Diagnosed

To build the best natural plan, you first need to know what is causing your tinnitus. At Audiology & Hearing Centers, a full tinnitus evaluation includes:

  • Hearing test
  • Ear examination
  • Questions about symptoms
  • Medical history review
  • Movement tests (jaw, neck, eyes)
  • Possible imaging (for rare cases)

Once we understand what is affecting your ears or auditory system, we can focus on the most effective care plan for your needs.

Daily Coping Tips for Tinnitus

Tinnitus becomes easier to manage when you build habits that support your emotional and physical health.

Helpful tips:

  • Keep a calm environment at home
  • Use soft background noise
  • Take short breaks during stress
  • Practice daily hydration
  • Get regular movement
  • Talk to loved ones about how you feel

Support is a powerful tool for healing.

Natural vs. Medical Options

Natural tools can provide significant relief, but sometimes medical treatment is needed, especially for:

  • Hearing loss
  • Earwax blockage
  • Jaw problems
  • High blood pressure
  • Medication side effects

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we often combine natural and medical strategies. Our goal is to find what works best for your ears, brain, and lifestyle.

Tinnitus Care at Audiology & Hearing Centers

Our clinic offers:

  • Full hearing evaluations
  • Tinnitus assessments
  • Hearing aids with tinnitus features
  • Sound therapy customization
  • Counseling and education
  • Long-term care plans

We help patients understand their tinnitus, build confidence, and feel more in control of their daily lives.

Final Thoughts: Finding What Works for You

If you’ve been searching for how to cure tinnitus naturally, remember this: while tinnitus may not have a single cure, many natural habits can reduce the ringing, calm your nervous system, and help you feel more balanced. Healthy choices like meditation, hydration, stress reduction, and sound therapy make a real difference. With the right support, tinnitus often becomes quieter, gentler, and easier to ignore.

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, our team is here to guide you through every step. We can help you understand your tinnitus, explore natural remedies, and build a tinnitus management plan that fits your life. 

If you are ready to learn more about how to cure tinnitus naturally and discover what works for your unique needs, schedule a visit with us today. You deserve relief, clarity, and better hearing, one step at a time.

data.meta_title: How to Service Hearing Aids: A Complete Guide to Care and Maintenance | Audiology and Hearing Centers
data.meta_description: Explore how to care for and service your hearing aids and why visits with an audiologist keep them working their best.
data.content:
Knowing how to service hearing aids properly keeps them working at their best and helps you avoid common problems. With a few simple daily habits and regular visits to an audiologist, you can extend the life of your devices and keep your hearing clear. This guide from Audiology & Hearing Centers explains how to clean, care for, and service your hearing aids at home-and when to seek professional help. 

Why Hearing Aid Care Matters 

Hearing aids are small but powerful devices that connect you to the people and sounds you love most. Like any tool you use every day, they need consistent care to stay in top condition. When maintained properly, they can last for years and deliver clear, reliable sound. 

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we often meet patients who are unsure about how to care for their hearing aids or when to schedule service. The good news is that simple, regular attention, combined with professional care, makes a huge difference in both performance and comfort. 

Daily Care at Home 

Spending just a few minutes each day caring for your hearing aids can prevent most issues. 

Here are simple steps you can take at home: 

Wipe them down nightly: 

Use a soft, dry cloth to gently clean your hearing aids before bed. This removes oils, dust, and moisture from the surface. 

Keep them dry: 

Moisture is one of the biggest causes of hearing aid failure. Always remove them before showering, swimming, or using hair spray. Store them in a dry, safe place when not in use. 

Check your batteries or charger: 

If your hearing aids use disposable batteries, replace them when you notice weak or distorted sound. If they’re rechargeable, make sure they’re placed in the charger each night. 

Clear away earwax: 

Earwax can block sound and affect performance. Use the small cleaning brush or wax tool that came with your devices to gently remove buildup around openings. 

These steps may seem simple, but they play a big role in keeping your hearing aids working properly every day. 

When to Perform Basic Service Yourself 

Some problems can be fixed at home without needing to visit a clinic. 

Try these steps before scheduling a professional service: 

No sound or weak sound:

Check if the battery is low or if the charger is properly connected. Replace or recharge as needed. 

Muffled sounds: 

Wax or debris could be blocking the sound openings. Clean them gently with the provided brush. 

Whistling or feedback: 

Ensure your hearing aid is sitting correctly in your ear. Sometimes a poor fit can cause unwanted noise. If these quick fixes don’t solve the issue, it’s time to see a professional for a full inspection. 

Audiology and Hearing Centers connects you to expert audiologists in Tampa Palms, Tampa Westchase, Venice, Palm Harbor, Sarasota, Bonita Springs, Ocala FL, and Alpharetta, GA, who can help you with a wide range of hearing concerns.

Why Professional Hearing Aid Service Is So Important 

Even with good home care, hearing aids need professional servicing to stay in top shape-just like cars need regular oil changes and tune-ups. A professional cleaning and adjustment can restore sound quality and prevent costly damage down the road. 

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, our audiologists and hearing aid specialists in Florida and Georgia provide the following professional services: 

Deep cleaning: 

Using special tools, we remove wax, dust, and debris that can’t be reached safely at home. 

Component check: 

We inspect all internal and external parts, including microphones, tubing, and speakers, to make sure everything is working correctly. 

Software and setting updates: 

Your hearing may change over time, and your environment might too. We adjust the programming to fit your current hearing needs. 

Minor repairs: 

If a part is damaged or worn out, we can often fix it in the office or send it to the manufacturer for professional repair. These visits keep your devices comfortable, clear, and reliable-so you can enjoy better hearing without interruption. 

Our professional services are available at convenient clinic locations in Tampa Palms, Tampa Westchase, Palm Harbor, Venice, Sarasota, FL, and Alpharetta, GA. 

How Often Should You Service Hearing Aids? 

We recommend scheduling a professional hearing aid service visit every six months to one year, even if your devices seem fine. This regular care helps us catch small problems early before they become major repairs. 

You should also schedule an appointment right away if you notice: 

  • No sound or sound cutting in and out 
  • Persistent feedback or whistling 
  • Difficulty charging or replacing batteries 
  • Physical damage to the casing or tubing 

Preventive care is the best way to protect your investment and keep your hearing consistent. 

The Role of the Audiologist in Hearing Aid Care 

Caring for hearing aids isn’t just about maintaining the devices, it’s about taking care of your hearing health as a whole. 

During a service visit, your audiologist can also: 

  • Re-test your hearing to detect any changes 
  • Refit your hearing aids if your ear shape or hearing needs have changed 
  • Answer your questions and show you the best daily cleaning techniques 
  • Recommend accessories or tools to make your hearing aids easier to maintain 

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, our audiologists combine clinical expertise with patient-centered care. We make sure your devices are performing well and that they continue to match your current hearing profile. 

Tips to Extend the Life of Your Hearing Aids 

Here are a few extra tips that can make your hearing aids last longer and perform better: 

Store them safely: 

Keep your hearing aids in a protective case or drying box when not in use. 

Avoid extreme heat or cold: 

Never leave them in direct sunlight, a hot car, or near heating vents. 

Handle with care: 

Hold them over a soft surface when cleaning or changing batteries to avoid damage from drops. 

Keep pets and small children away: 

Hearing aids are small and can be tempting to chew or play with. 

Clean your ears regularly: 

Reducing earwax buildup naturally helps your hearing aids perform better. 

Taking these precautions will protect your hearing aids and help you get consistent, high-quality sound every day. 

Keeping Your Hearing Aids and Your Hearing Healthy 

Your hearing aids are a valuable part of your daily life. With the right care and professional service, they can help you stay connected, active, and confident. At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we provide both expert hearing aid maintenance and full hearing health services. 

From cleaning and repairs to hearing tests and new fittings, our team ensures you always hear your best. 

When you visit our offices in Tampa Palms, Tampa Westchase, Palm Harbor, Venice, Sarasota, FL, or Alpharetta, GA, you’ll receive personalized attention from specialists who care about your comfort and hearing success. 

Schedule Your Hearing Aid Service Appointment 

If you’re unsure how to service your hearing aids or it’s been more than six months since your last visit, now is the perfect time to schedule an appointment. 

Our Florida and Georgia audiologists at Audiology & Hearing Centers are here to help you maintain your hearing aids and hearing health. 

From simple cleanings to full repairs, we make the process easy, affordable, and effective. 

Call us today or request an appointment online to schedule your next hearing aid service visit. 

With proper care and expert guidance, your hearing aids will continue to provide clear, comfortable sound and keep you connected to the world around you.

data.meta_title: How to Use Earwax Removal Drops Safely and Effectively | Audiology and Hearing Centers
data.meta_description: Learn how to use earwax removal drops safely and when to see an audiologist. Schedule an ear exam with Audiology & Hearing Centers today.
data.content:
• Earwax removal drops can help soften earwax, but only when used the right way.
  • Using drops incorrectly can push wax deeper or cause irritation.
  • If symptoms continue, an audiologist can safely remove earwax and check your hearing.

Earwax plays an important role in keeping your ears healthy, but too much buildup can cause discomfort, muffled hearing, and pressure. Many people turn to earwax removal drops at home and ask the same question: how to use earwax removal drops safely without causing more problems.

In this guide, we explain what earwax removal drops do, how to use them step by step, when they are helpful, and when it’s best to see an audiologist for professional earwax removal. Knowing the right approach can protect your ears and help you avoid unnecessary hearing problems.

Audiology & Hearing Centers is here to help you understand your ear health and know when home care is enough, and when professional care is the safer choice.

What Is Earwax and Why Do We Have It?

Earwax, also called cerumen, is made by glands in the ear canal. It may look unpleasant, but it actually serves a purpose.

Earwax helps:

  • Trap dust, dirt, and debris
  • Protect the ear canal from infection
  • Keep the ear canal from becoming too dry

In most people, earwax slowly moves out of the ear on its own. Jaw movement from talking or chewing helps push old wax outward. Problems start when wax builds up faster than it can clear.

When Earwax Becomes a Problem

Too much earwax can cause a blockage, also called impacted earwax. This can happen for many reasons, including:

  • Narrow ear canals
  • Frequent earbud or hearing aid use
  • Using cotton swabs or Q-tips
  • Dry or hard wax
  • Certain skin conditions

Signs of earwax buildup include:

  • Muffled or reduced hearing
  • Ear fullness or pressure
  • Ringing in the ears
  • Itching or discomfort
  • Trouble hearing conversations

When these symptoms appear, many people reach for earwax removal drops.

What Are Earwax Removal Drops?

Earwax removal drops are liquids placed in the ear to help soften and loosen wax. They do not “wash” wax out. Instead, they make the wax softer so it can move out naturally or be removed more easily by a professional.

Common types of earwax removal drops include:

  • Hydrogen peroxide–based drops
  • Carbamide peroxide drops
  • Saline solutions
  • Mineral oil or olive oil–based drops

Each type works a little differently, but the goal is the same: soften hardened earwax.

How Do Earwax Removal Drops Work?

When drops reach the earwax, they break it down or soften it. Some drops bubble or fizz. This is normal and means the drops are working.

However, softened wax still needs a way out. If wax is deeply impacted, drops alone may not solve the problem.

That’s why knowing how to use earwax removal drops correctly is so important.

If you believe OTC wax removal drops can’t help you, it’s time to see an audiologist for expert care. Audiology and Hearing Centers offer professional earwax removal in Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Sarasota, Bonita Springs, FL, and Alpharetta, GA.

How to Use Earwax Removal Drops Safely

Follow these general steps unless your product instructions say otherwise.

Step 1: Read the Instructions

Always read the label first. Some drops are used once a day, others twice a day, and some only for a few days.

Step 2: Warm the Bottle

Cold drops can cause dizziness. Hold the bottle in your hand for a minute to warm it slightly.

Step 3: Tilt Your Head

Tilt your head so the affected ear faces upward.

Step 4: Apply the Drops

Place the recommended number of drops into the ear. Do not touch the dropper to your ear.

Step 5: Stay Still

Remain in this position for 3–5 minutes. You may hear fizzing or popping sounds. This is normal.

Step 6: Drain Gently

Tilt your head the opposite way and let excess liquid drain out. Wipe the outer ear only.

Step 7: Repeat as Directed

Some wax needs several days of treatment. Do not overuse the drops.

What NOT to Do After Using Earwax Removal Drops

  • Do not use cotton swabs or Q-tips
  • Do not insert tools, hairpins, or tissues
  • Do not flush your ear unless directed by a professional
  • Do not force water into the ear

These actions can push wax deeper and increase the risk of injury.

When Earwax Removal Drops Are NOT Safe

Do not use earwax removal drops if you have:

  • Ear pain
  • Drainage from the ear
  • A known or suspected eardrum hole
  • A history of ear surgery
  • An active ear infection

If you are unsure, it’s best to check with an audiologist or doctor first.

Why Earwax Removal Drops Sometimes Don’t Work

Drops may not help if:

  • Wax is fully impacted
  • Wax is very dry or hard
  • The ear canal is swollen
  • The blockage is not earwax

In these cases, continued use can make symptoms worse.

The Risk of DIY Earwax Removal

Many people try home flushing with warm water, hydrogen peroxide, or olive oil. While these methods may help some people, they also carry risks when done incorrectly.

Possible risks include:

  • Ear canal irritation
  • Dizziness
  • Infection
  • Eardrum damage
  • Pushing wax deeper

That’s why professional earwax removal is often the safest option.

How Audiologists Remove Earwax Safely

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, earwax removal is done using safe, medical tools by trained professionals.

Common methods include:

  • Gentle suction
  • Specialized tools designed for the ear
  • Careful irrigation when appropriate

These methods allow clear removal without harming the ear canal or eardrum.

Earwax and Hearing Aids

If you wear hearing aids, earwax buildup is more common. Hearing aids can block natural wax movement and push wax deeper.

Signs wax may affect hearing aids:

  • Weak or distorted sound
  • Whistling or feedback
  • Hearing aids cutting in and out

Regular ear checks help keep hearing aids working properly.

How Often Should You Use Earwax Removal Drops?

There is no set schedule for everyone.

In general:

  • Use drops only when symptoms appear
  • Do not use daily as a routine
  • Avoid long-term use without guidance

If wax returns often, an audiologist can help create a safe plan.

Earwax Removal Drops vs. Professional Care

Earwax removal drops may help with mild buildup, but they are not a replacement for an ear exam.

Professional care:

  • Confirms wax is the problem
  • Removes wax safely
  • Protects hearing
  • Prevents repeated blockage

When to See an Audiologist

Make an appointment if:

  • Symptoms last more than a few days
  • Hearing feels suddenly worse
  • You feel pain, pressure, or dizziness
  • Drops do not improve symptoms
  • You wear hearing aids

Audiology & Hearing Centers offers professional earwax removal and hearing evaluations to help you hear clearly again.

Ear Health Tips for the Future

  • Avoid cotton swabs
  • Dry ears gently after bathing
  • Use hearing aid cleaning tools
  • Schedule regular ear checks
  • Treat hearing changes early

Schedule A Professional Ear Wax Removal Today

Understanding how to use earwax removal drops can help protect your ears and prevent unnecessary hearing problems. Drops can be helpful when used carefully and correctly, but they are not always the right solution.

If symptoms continue or return often, professional care is the safest choice. At Audiology & Hearing Centers, our team is here to help you decide whether earwax removal drops are enough or whether in-office care is the better option.

If you have questions about earwax buildup or need safe earwax removal, contact Audiology & Hearing Centers today to schedule an appointment.

Learning how to use earwax removal drops the right way is important, but knowing when to seek expert care matters even more.

data.meta_title: Hyperacusis: Understanding Sound Sensitivity | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Discover symptoms, causes, and treatments for hyperacusis. Learn how audiologists can help you cope with sound sensitivity.
data.content:
Does everyday sound seem painfully loud or overwhelming? You might be experiencing a condition known as hyperacusis. People with hyperacusis often feel discomfort or even pain when exposed to regular sounds like running water, traffic noise, or clinking dishes. 

This condition can make social situations, work environments, and daily life extremely challenging. Fortunately, hyperacusis is treatable. 

At Audiology & Hearing Center, our experienced audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL, are here to help you find relief through proper diagnosis and care. 

Understanding the signs and seeking timely treatment can make a world of difference.

Hyperacusis: Signs and Symptoms

Hyperacusis affects how your brain processes sound, causing ordinary noise levels to feel uncomfortably or unbearably loud. It’s different from normal hearing sensitivity and should not be ignored. Common signs include:

  • Pain or discomfort from everyday sounds like laughter, dishwashers, or car engines
  • A sense of fullness or pressure in the ears without a physical cause
  • Tension, stress, or anxiety triggered by noise
  • Avoidance of social gatherings or public places due to noise fear
  • Trouble concentrating due to distracting sounds

At Audiology & Hearing Center, we help patients who struggle with sound sensitivity. 

Recognizing these symptoms early can help you get the right treatment and restore comfort to your daily life.

Hyperacusis: Causes

Hyperacusis can develop gradually or suddenly and may stem from a variety of sources. Some known causes include:

  • Loud noise exposure: Prolonged exposure to loud environments such as concerts, construction sites, or gunfire
  • Head injuries: Trauma to the head or ear can disrupt normal sound processing
  • Medical conditions: Bell’s palsy, Lyme disease, TMJ disorders, and migraines are sometimes associated with hyperacusis
  • Ear surgeries: Some patients experience changes in sound tolerance after surgical procedures
  • Chronic stress or anxiety: Mental health conditions can exacerbate auditory sensitivity

Each person’s case is unique, which is why getting a thorough assessment by an audiologist is very important.

Diagnosing Hyperacusis

Diagnosing hyperacusis involves more than just a hearing test. At Audiology & Hearing Center, we use a comprehensive approach to evaluate your sound tolerance and rule out other hearing conditions. 

This may include loudness discomfort level (LDL) testing and a detailed patient history. 

Accurate diagnosis is key to successful treatment, and our experienced audiologists will guide you every step of the way. Don’t try to tough it out—getting expert help can change your relationship with sound for the better.

Hyperacusis Treatment

While there is no one-size-fits-all cure for hyperacusis, many effective treatment options exist. Some patients benefit from:

  • Sound therapy: Gradual exposure to soft sounds to desensitize the auditory system
  • Tinnitus Retraining Therapy (TRT): Helpful if you also experience ringing in the ears
  • Counseling or cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT): Helps manage the emotional effects of hyperacusis
  • Custom ear protection: Filters out harmful noise while allowing safe sound levels

Our team at Audiology & Hearing Center customizes treatment based on your needs, helping you regain control over your sound environment.

Hyperacusis Home Remedies

There are a variety of home remedies and lifestyle changes people try to reduce hyperacusis symptoms. These include:

  • Avoiding noisy environments: While it’s tempting to use earplugs all the time, doing so may increase sensitivity in the long run. Use protection only when truly needed.
  • Relaxation techniques: Deep breathing, yoga, or meditation may help reduce anxiety related to sound.
  • Creating a calm home sound environment: Using white noise machines, soft music, or nature sounds can help your brain feel less startled by sharp noises.
  • Healthy lifestyle habits: Regular exercise, good sleep, and a balanced diet may support overall auditory health.
  • Gradual sound exposure: Slowly reintroducing gentle background sounds can help reduce sensitivity over time.

While these strategies may bring relief, they are not a substitute for professional care. It is highly recommended to see an audiologist to accurately diagnose and treat hyperacusis. 

Hyperacusis Risk Factors

Anyone can develop hyperacusis, but certain factors can increase the risk:

  • Frequent exposure to loud environments: Musicians, factory workers, and construction workers are especially at risk
  • Existing ear conditions: Tinnitus, hearing loss, or ear infections may contribute to hyperacusis
  • Neurological issues: Conditions like autism spectrum disorder or head trauma can affect how the brain processes sound
  • Mental health: Anxiety, stress, and depression are often associated with increased sound sensitivity
  • Use of certain medications: Some drugs can heighten auditory perception as a side effect

Being aware of these risk factors can help you take proactive steps. If you fall into one of these categories and notice any changes in your hearing, reach out to us at Audiology & Hearing Center.

Audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL

Hyperacusis can make everyday life feel overwhelming, but you don’t have to navigate it alone. With the right diagnosis and personalized care, you can manage your symptoms and regain control over your sound environment. 

If you or a loved one is experiencing unusual sound sensitivity, ourexpert audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL are here to help.

Contact us today to schedule a consultation and start your journey toward a more peaceful, sound-balanced life.

Our clinics are located in Tampa Palms, FL, Tampa Westchase, FL, Palm Harbor, FL, and Venice, FL.

data.meta_title: Is It Good to Get Your Ears Professionally Cleaned? | Audiology and Hearing Centers
data.meta_description: Wondering if professional ear cleaning is right for you? Learn the benefits, safety, and local options. Schedule your visit today.
data.content:
Key Takeaways

  • Professional ear cleaning is safe, gentle, and effective when done by trained clinicians.
  • It can improve hearing, hearing aid performance, and overall ear health.
  • Clinic-based methods are safer than home kits, swabs, or candles.

Why Earwax Builds Up in the First Place

Earwax is not dirty. It protects the ear canal by:

  • Trapping dust and debris
  • Keeping skin moisturized
  • Reducing infection risk

Problems happen when earwax does not move out on its own.

Common causes of buildup include:

  • Narrow or curved ear canals
  • Aging skin that does not shed easily
  • Hearing aid or earbud use
  • Using cotton swabs that push wax deeper

When wax builds up, it can cause blockage and symptoms.

Common Symptoms of Earwax Blockage

Earwax blockage can feel different for each person. Common signs include:

  • Muffled or reduced hearing
  • Ear fullness or pressure
  • Itching or irritation
  • Ringing in the ears
  • Drainage or odor
  • Hearing aid feedback or poor sound quality

If these symptoms last more than a few days, professional care is recommended. Audiology and Hearing Centers offer professional earwax removal in Alpharetta, GA, Palm Harbor, FL, Sarasota, FL, and other key locations.

Is It Good to Get Your Ears Professionally Cleaned if You Wear Hearing Aids?

Yes, professional ear cleaning is especially important for hearing aid users.

Hearing aids:

  • Block natural wax movement
  • Push wax deeper into the ear canal
  • Stop sound if wax covers the receiver

Can Professional Ear Cleaning Improve Hearing Aid Performance?

Yes. Cleaning removes wax that:

  • Blocks sound from reaching the eardrum
  • Causes feedback or whistling
  • Damages hearing aid parts

Many patients notice instant improvement after wax removal.

Professional Ear Wax Removal vs. Over-the-Counter Kits: Which Is Safer?

Professional care is safer because the ear canal and eardrum are delicate.

Home kits may:

  • Miss deep wax
  • Push wax closer to the eardrum
  • Increase infection risk
  • Cause skin cuts or swelling

Professional care uses training, tools, and visual guidance.

Comparison Table: Ear Wax Removal Methods

What Is Microsuction and Why Is It Preferred?

Microsuction is a dry, gentle method that uses controlled suction while the clinician looks directly into the ear.

Benefits include:

  • No water
  • No pressure on the eardrum
  • Clear view of the ear canal
  • Fast relief

Microsuction is often the safest choice for seniors and hearing aid users.

Is Professional Ear Cleaning Safe for Seniors With Thinning Ear Canal Skin?

Yes, when done by trained professionals.

As we age:

  • Ear canal skin becomes thinner
  • Blood vessels are closer to the surface
  • Injury risk increases with home tools

Professional providers adjust technique based on:

  • Skin condition
  • Wax type
  • Medical history

This protects comfort and safety.

Does Professional Ear Cleaning Hurt?

No. Professional ear cleaning should not hurt.

Patients may feel:

  • Mild pressure
  • A tickling sensation
  • Cool or warm water (if irrigation is used)

Pain is not normal and is a sign to stop. Trained clinicians monitor comfort throughout the visit. We serve patients needing safe earwax removal in Roswell, GA and Cumming, GA in our Alpharetta office.

How Much Does Ear Wax Removal Cost at Audiology & Hearing Centers?

Costs vary by location and method, but most professional cleanings fall within a reasonable range.

Typical factors include:

  • Severity of blockage
  • One ear or both ears
  • Method used (microsuction or irrigation)

Our team can explain pricing clearly before treatment.

Do I Need an Appointment, or Do You Take Walk-Ins?

Most locations recommend scheduling an appointment to:

  • Reduce wait time
  • Ensure the right equipment is available
  • Allow proper assessment

Some locations may accommodate walk-ins based on availability. Calling ahead is best.

Why Professional Ear Cleaning Is Better Than “Do Nothing”

Ignoring earwax buildup can lead to:

  • Worsening hearing loss
  • Increased tinnitus
  • Repeated infections
  • Poor hearing aid performance

Professional cleaning addresses the issue safely before complications start.

How Often Should Ears Be Professionally Cleaned?

There is no one-size-fits-all schedule.

Some patients need cleaning:

  • Once a year
  • Every 6 months
  • More often if they wear hearing aids

Your provider will recommend a plan based on your ear health.

What Happens During a Professional Ear Cleaning Appointment?

A typical visit includes:

  • Visual ear exam
  • Assessment of wax type and location
  • Safe removal using the best method
  • Post-cleaning ear check

Most appointments take 20–30 minutes.

Can Professional Ear Cleaning Improve Hearing?

Yes. Removing wax often:

  • Restores sound clarity
  • Reduces pressure
  • Improves hearing test results

If hearing does not improve, further assessment may be recommended.

When Earwax Is Not the Only Problem

Sometimes symptoms are not caused by wax alone.

Other possible causes include:

  • Middle ear fluid
  • Infection
  • Hearing loss related to aging or noise exposure

Professional care ensures the correct diagnosis.

Providing Safe Earwax Removal in Florida and Georgia

We happily serve patients in:

  • Palm Harbor, FL
  • Tampa Westchase, FL
  • Sarasota, FL
  • Bonita Springs, FL
  • Alpharetta, GA

Benefit from having trusted local providers who understand regional patient needs and offer consistent follow-up care.

Why Choose Audiology & Hearing Centers?

Our clinics focus on:

  • Evidence-based audiology care
  • Gentle, patient-first treatment

data.meta_title: Misophonia Treatment | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Discover expert evaluation and treatment for misophonia in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL at Audiology & Hearing Center.
data.content:
Does the sound of chewing, tapping, or breathing cause intense irritation or anxiety? If so, you may be experiencing a condition known as misophonia. At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we understand how this sound sensitivity can interfere with everyday life. 

Our audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL, offer compassionate evaluation and evidence-based treatments to help you manage misophonia and restore comfort in your daily routine.

Overview of Misophonia

Misophonia is a neurological condition where certain everyday sounds trigger extreme emotional reactions like anger, anxiety, or panic. Unlike general noise sensitivity, people with Misophonia often have a strong emotional and physical response to specific “trigger” sounds, such as:

  • Chewing or slurping
  • Pen clicking or foot tapping
  • Breathing or throat clearing
  • Clock ticking or repetitive tapping

These sounds, often harmless to others, can be unbearable to someone with Misophonia.

Importance of Early Evaluation and Treatment

Early diagnosis is key to managing the condition effectively. Without treatment, Misophonia can disrupt social interactions, limit professional opportunities, and take a toll on emotional well-being. Seeking help early allows for better symptom management and improved quality of life.

Impact on Daily Life

For individuals with misophonia, common environments like classrooms, offices, restaurants, or public transit can become overwhelming. This condition often causes people to avoid social gatherings, experience strained relationships, or develop workplace difficulties due to their reactions to sounds.

Misophonia Causes and Risk Factors

The exact cause of Misophonia remains unclear, but researchers believe it involves abnormal connections between the brain’s auditory system and areas that regulate emotions. Potential contributing factors include:

  • Neurological miswiring in the auditory cortex and limbic system
  • Previous trauma or negative sound associations
  • Auditory overprocessing linked to other sensory disorders

Who is at Risk?

Misophonia can develop in both children and adults, though symptoms often appear during late childhood or adolescence. It may be more common among:

  • Individuals with anxiety disorders
  • Those with obsessive-compulsive tendencies
  • People with sensory processing disorders or autism spectrum conditions

Genetic predispositions and environmental factors may also increase susceptibility.

Misophonia Diagnosis

Diagnosing Misophonia involves a thorough evaluation by a qualified professional, such as an audiologist. The process may include:

  • A detailed patient history
  • Use of questionnaires to assess symptom patterns
  • Sound exposure tests to identify specific triggers
  • Evaluation of co-occurring conditions like anxiety or hyperacusis

Our audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL, offer compassionate evaluation and evidence-based treatments to help you manage misophonia and restore comfort in your daily routine.

The Role of Audiologists

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, our audiologists are specially trained to identify and manage auditory-related conditions. We take a holistic, patient-centered approach—helping individuals recognize triggers, validate their experiences, and begin effective treatment tailored to their needs.

Treatment Options for Misophonia

Sound Therapy

Sound therapy involves desensitizing the brain’s response to triggers. This may include:

  • White noise generators or sound maskers
  • Progressive sound exposure to reduce hypersensitivity
  • Custom ear-level devices to create soothing background noise

This approach helps reduce the emotional impact of trigger sounds over time.

Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)

CBT is a common psychological intervention that teaches patients to reframe their emotional reactions. With a licensed therapist, individuals learn coping strategies to reduce:

  • Emotional overreactions
  • Avoidance behaviors
  • Anticipatory anxiety

CBT has shown success in helping people with Misophonia regain control over their responses.

Lifestyle Modifications

Simple adjustments can also make a significant difference:

  • Using earplugs or noise-canceling headphones
  • Creating quiet zones at home or work
  • Practicing relaxation techniques like deep breathing or mindfulness
  • Communicating clearly with loved ones about your needs

Other Treatment Approaches

Additional options may include:

  • Medications to manage anxiety or related conditions (prescribed by a healthcare provider)
  • Biofeedback therapy to control stress responses
  • Support groups or online communities for shared understanding

FAQs about Misophonia

What causes misophonia?

While the exact cause is unknown, it’s believed to stem from abnormal brain responses to sound, possibly involving genetics and emotional processing regions of the brain.

Can misophonia be cured?

There’s no permanent cure, but many individuals experience relief through sound therapy, counseling, and lifestyle changes.

How do I know if I have misophonia or just a sensitivity to noise?

If specific sounds cause intense emotional or physical reactions that interfere with daily life, it may be Misophonia rather than general noise sensitivity.

How can sound therapy help with misophonia?

Sound therapy can train your brain to respond less emotionally to trigger sounds, easing your overall distress and improving tolerance.

Do I need to see an audiologist for misophonia?

Yes. An audiologist can evaluate your hearing, identify triggers, and provide sound-based therapies or refer you to additional specialists as needed.

Why Choose Audiology & Hearing Centers?

Experience and Expertise

With decades of experience in auditory health, our team has successfully helped patients of all ages manage sound sensitivity issues, including Misophonia. We are committed to staying up to date with the latest treatments and research.

Personalized Treatment Plans

Each patient receives a customized care plan based on their symptoms, history, and lifestyle. We take the time to understand your specific needs and work collaboratively to find solutions that work for you.

Advanced Technology

Our clinics use state-of-the-art diagnostic tools and sound therapy devices to deliver modern, effective care. From tinnitus evaluations to real-ear measurements, we provide precise testing and tailored recommendations.

Misophonia Evaluation and Treatment in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL

If you or a loved one is struggling with misophonia, you don’t have to suffer in silence. 

Audiology & Hearing Centers is here to help you take back control of your life with compassionate care and proven treatment strategies.

Schedule a Misophonia evaluation with our audiologists in Tampa Bay or Venice, FL.

data.meta_title: Why an Online Hearing Test Can't Replace a Visit to an Audiologist | Audiology and Hearing Centers
data.meta_description: Learn the pros and cons of online hearing tests and why visiting an audiologist gives you the best care for lasting hearing health.
data.content:
Many people use an online hearing test when they notice changes in their hearing. These tests can give a quick idea of possible hearing loss, but they cannot provide a complete or accurate picture of your ear health. At Audiology and Hearing Centers, we explain what online hearing tests can and cannot do-and why a professional hearing evaluation with an audiologist is always the safest choice. 

Understanding Online Hearing Tests 

Today, it’s common for people to look for answers online, especially when they start to notice hearing problems. A simple search can lead you to an online hearing test, which promises quick results in just a few minutes. These online tools are usually easy to use. You listen to a series of tones, words, or speech through your computer, tablet, or phone and select what you hear. 

In most cases, they give a rough idea of whether your hearing might have changed. For someone who is just curious or wondering if they should seek help, an online hearing test can be a helpful first step. It can raise awareness about hearing health and encourage people to take action. That’s a good thing, but it’s not the full story. 

What Online Hearing Tests Can Do 

An online hearing test offers some convenience and accessibility benefits: 

Quick results: 

You can take it anytime from home and get immediate feedback. 

Free or low cost: 

Most online tests are free, making them easy to try. 

Early awareness: 

If the test shows hearing difficulties, it might motivate someone to see an audiologist. 

At Audiology and Hearing Centers in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL, we agree that awareness is an important first step. 

Recognizing that your hearing has changed is the key to protecting it. However, online tests can only go so far. 

We provide comprehensive hearing tests in Tampa Palms, Tampa Westchase, Venice, Palm Harbor, Sarasota, Bonita Springs, Ocala FL, and Alpharetta, GA.

The Limits of Online Hearing Tests 

Online hearing tests have major limitations. While they may help identify possible issues, they leave out critical details that only a professional evaluation can find. 

No view of your ear health: 

Online tools can’t look inside your ear canal to check for earwax, fluid, or infection, which are common causes of hearing changes. 

Accuracy varies: 

Results depend on your device, headphones, and even background noise, which can make the test unreliable. 

Misses medical connections: 

Hearing loss can be linked to other conditions like diabetes, high blood pressure, or prolonged noise exposure. Online tests can’t measure these risks. 

No personal guidance: 

A computer program can’t discuss your lifestyle, answer questions, or recommend the right solutions for your needs. This is why an online hearing test should never replace a full hearing evaluation with a trained audiologist. 

At Audiology and Hearing Centers, we provide professional, in-person hearing tests at our clinics in Tampa Palms, Tampa Westchase, Venice, Palm Harbor, FL, and Alpharetta, GA. 

Why Seeing an Audiologist Matters 

An audiologist is a hearing care expert who does far more than measure how well you hear. They take the time to understand your health, history, and daily challenges. 

During a hearing test in one of our Tampa Bay or Venice offices, our audiologists will: 

  • Examine your ears to check for wax buildup, fluid, or infection. 
  • Use advanced equipment in a quiet, controlled space to measure your hearing at different pitches and volumes. 
  • Explain your test results clearly, so you understand exactly what’s happening. 
  • Discuss personalized solutions-whether that means hearing aids, treatment, or other hearing care options. 

This complete approach ensures that you get answers that are accurate, professional, and tailored to you. 

When You Should Schedule a Professional Hearing Test 

Even if an online hearing test says your hearing is “normal,” you should schedule a professional exam if you notice: 

  • Difficulty following conversations, especially in background noise 
  • Turning up the TV or radio louder than others prefer 
  • Feeling like people are mumbling 
  • Ringing or buzzing sounds in your ears (tinnitus) 
  • Asking others to repeat themselves often 

These signs can mean you’re experiencing hearing changes-even if an online test doesn’t show it. The only way to know for sure is with a complete hearing evaluation from an audiologist. 

What to Expect During a Professional Hearing Test 

At Audiology and Hearing Centers, we make the process simple and comfortable. Our audiologists will: 

Start with a conversation: 

We’ll ask about your health, symptoms, and lifestyle. 

Check your ears: 

Using a small camera or otoscope, we’ll make sure your ear canal is clear. 

Perform the hearing test: 

You’ll listen to different tones and words through high-quality equipment in a soundproof room. 

Review your results: 

We’ll explain your hearing levels and what they mean for your everyday life. 

Discuss next steps: 

If hearing aids or other treatments could help, we’ll guide you through your best options. 

Our goal is to provide not just results, but understanding, care, and solutions designed around you. 

Hearing Tests in Florida and Georgia 

Your hearing connects you to the people and moments that matter most. While an online hearing test can offer a quick snapshot, it cannot replace the care, accuracy, and personal support of a full evaluation. 

At Audiology and Hearing Centers, our compassionate team provides complete hearing testing and hearing aid services in: 

  • Tampa Palms, FL 
  • Tampa Westchase, FL 
  • Palm Harbor, FL 
  • Venice, FL 
  • Alpharetta, GA 
  • Bonita Springs, FL
  • Ocala, FL
  • Sarasota, FL

Each visit is designed to give you the most accurate answers about your hearing and help you make confident choices for your health. 

Why Professional Hearing Care Makes a Difference 

When you visit an audiologist, you’re not just getting a test; you’re getting a partner in your long-term hearing wellness. 

Professional testing can reveal: 

  • The type and degree of hearing loss you have 
  • Whether your hearing loss is temporary or permanent 
  • The safest, most effective solutions for better hearing 

At our clinics, we believe in compassionate, patient-centered care. We take time to explain, educate, and empower you so you understand your hearing health from every angle. 

Schedule a Hearing Test with Audiology and Hearing Centers 

If you’ve taken an online hearing test or have started to notice changes in your hearing, it’s time for a professional evaluation. At Audiology and Hearing Centers, we offer expert hearing care in Florida. 

Our audiologists and hearing aid specialists are here to listen, answer your questions, and help you find the best path toward better hearing. Your hearing health deserves more than a quick online result – it deserves personal care, accuracy, and expert guidance. 

Call us today or request an appointment online to schedule your hearing test. You’ll leave with clear answers, peace of mind, and a plan for better hearing.

data.meta_title: Over the Counter Hearing Aids | Audiology & Hearing Centers
data.meta_description: Learn the pros and cons of over the counter hearing aids and why seeing an audiologist in Tampa Bay or Venice, FL gives you better results.
data.content:
Hearing aids are no longer found only in an audiologist’s office. In recent years, over the counter (OTC) hearing aids have become available at stores and online. These devices are often advertised as quick, affordable, and easy solutions for people with mild hearing loss.

If you have started noticing changes in your hearing, you may be tempted to try OTC devices before seeing a professional. While the convenience can seem appealing, hearing loss is a personal and medical issue that deserves careful attention. 

At Audiology and Hearing Centers in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL, we believe it is important to understand both the benefits and the limits of over the counter hearing aids before making a decision.

What Are Over the Counter Hearing Aids?

OTC hearing aids are designed for adults with perceived mild to moderate hearing loss. Unlike prescription devices, they do not require a hearing test or a fitting by a licensed audiologist. You can walk into a store, buy them off the shelf, or order them online.

Some come with rechargeable batteries and smartphone apps that let you adjust the volume or change listening programs. On the surface, this sounds simple and easy. For certain people, they may provide a small boost in hearing.

Why Many People Consider Them

The biggest reason people try OTC hearing aids is cost. They are usually less expensive than prescription hearing aids. They are also convenient, since no appointment is needed. If someone only struggles in a few situations, such as in restaurants or when watching TV, they may see OTC hearing aids as a quick fix.

Where OTC Hearing Aids Fall Short

While OTC hearing aids may seem like a solution, they often do not meet the unique needs of each person. Hearing loss is not one-size-fits-all. Some of the most common challenges include:

  • No diagnostic testing: Without a hearing test, you do not know the type or cause of your hearing loss. It could be something simple, like earwax, or more complex, such as inner ear damage or even a medical condition.
  • Limited adjustments: OTC devices amplify sound, but they are not programmed for your personal hearing levels. This can make speech sound unclear or background noise overwhelming.
  • Fit and comfort issues: Everyone’s ears are shaped differently. An ill-fitting device can cause soreness or feedback (whistling sounds).
  • Lack of ongoing care: Hearing aids are not a one-time purchase. Most people need follow-up visits for cleaning, fine-tuning, and counseling to get the most benefit. OTC devices do not come with that support.

Why Seeing an Audiologist Matters

When you visit an audiologist, you receive more than a device. You receive care that is tailored to your health and lifestyle. At Audiology and Hearing Centers, we provide:

  • Comprehensive hearing evaluations to measure the type and degree of your hearing loss
  • Personalized fittings that make sure your devices are comfortable and effective
  • Real ear measurements to confirm that sound is being delivered correctly in your ear canal
  • Ongoing support through follow-up visits, cleanings, and adjustments
  • Trusted guidance for managing tinnitus, balance concerns, or sudden changes in hearing

This level of care cannot be found in a store or on a website.

The Bigger Picture of Hearing Loss

Hearing loss affects much more than the ability to listen. It can impact relationships, social confidence, safety, and even brain health. Research shows that untreated hearing loss can lead to memory problems, isolation, and a higher risk of falls.

Hearing is deeply connected to your overall well-being. That is why it deserves professional care, not just a quick purchase.

Are OTC Hearing Aids Ever Helpful?

For some adults with very mild hearing difficulties, an OTC device may offer a small improvement. But if you find yourself asking people to repeat themselves often, struggling in noisy places, or feeling like sounds are muffled, it is best to see an audiologist. Only a full evaluation can determine whether your hearing loss is mild, moderate, or more complex.

Take the First Step

data.meta_title: Pressure in Ear: Causes, Relief & When to See an Audiologist | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Feeling pressure in your ear? Learn the common causes and why seeing an audiologist can help you find relief.
data.content:
Have you ever felt your ears were blocked or full—like they needed to “pop” but wouldn’t? That uncomfortable sensation is known as ear pressure, and it can feel like fullness, tightness, or aching in the ear, sometimes accompanied by ear pain, muffled hearing, or even dizziness.

While it’s common and often temporary, persistent or recurring pressure in the ear can signal a more serious problem. Since this symptom may be linked to issues in the outer ear, middle ear, inner ear, or even related areas like the sinuses or jaw, it’s important to have it checked out by a professional—especially an audiologist. 

Audiology & Hearing Center connects you to expert audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL.

What Causes Pressure in the Ear?

1. Ear Wax Buildup

One of the most common causes of clogged ears and ear discomfort is an excessive buildup of ear wax in the ear canal. When ear wax becomes compacted, it can block sound, create a feeling of fullness, and affect pressure in the external ear.

Many people try home remedies like cotton swabs or ear candles, but these can make the issue worse by pushing wax deeper or causing injury. That’s why professional ear wax removal from an audiologist is always the safest and most effective solution.

Audiology & Hearing Center provides professional ear wax removal in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL.

2. Eustachian Tube Dysfunction

The Eustachian tubes connect the middle ear to the back of the throat and help regulate ear pressure by allowing air to flow in and out. If these tubes become blocked—due to allergies, sinus infections, or colds—they can’t equalize pressure properly.

This can result in muffled hearing, a sensation of clogged ears, or popping sounds when you swallow or yawn. Chronic dysfunction may require medical intervention and specialized treatment from an audiologist.

Audiology & Hearing Center connects you to expert audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL.

3. Middle Ear Infections (Otitis Media)

Fluid buildup from an infection in the middle ear can cause ear pressure, pain, and sometimes fever or drainage. These infections are especially common in children but can affect adults too—especially after upper respiratory illnesses.

Recurring infections can damage hearing and should never be ignored. If you frequently feel ear pain or pressure, an audiologist can assess whether fluid or infection is affecting your middle ear.

4. Barotrauma

Flying, diving, or even driving through mountains can create sudden changes in air pressure that your ears may struggle to adjust to. This is called barotrauma, and it can feel like popping, fullness, or pain in the ears.

While these symptoms usually go away after equalizing pressure, if ear pressure lingers after a trip, it’s time to see an audiologist for evaluation.

5. Meniere’s Disease

This chronic inner ear condition is associated with a buildup of fluid in the inner ear, which affects both pressure and balance. A hallmark symptom of Meniere’s Disease is the sensation of ear fullness, often accompanied by hearing loss, tinnitus (ringing), and vertigo.

Audiologists play a key role in diagnosing and managing Meniere’s, especially by monitoring hearing and helping with balance-related symptoms.

6. Other Causes of Ear Pressure

Other conditions that may lead to pressure in the ear include:

  • Temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorders
  • Structural abnormalities in the ear
  • Acoustic neuromas (benign tumors along the hearing nerve)

Because the causes can range from minor to serious, it’s best to start with a professional assessment from an audiologist.

Why You Should See an Audiologist for Ear Pressure

1. Accurate Diagnosis

Audiologists have the tools and expertise to identify the underlying cause of your ear pressure, whether it’s wax buildup, fluid, or something more complex. Testing includes visual inspection, tympanometry, hearing tests, and more.

2. Personalized Treatment

No two ears are the same! Your audiologist can tailor your treatment—whether that means ear wax removal, suggesting Eustachian tube exercises, or referring you to an ENT for further care.

3. Hearing Protection and Monitoring

If ear pressure is affecting your hearing, audiologists can monitor your hearing levels and help prevent further damage—especially important in chronic cases like Meniere’s or recurrent infections.

4. Comprehensive Care

Audiologists don’t just diagnose—they stay with you through follow-up and treatment to ensure your ear health improves and stays on track.

Common Treatments for Ear Pressure

Professional Ear Wax Removal

At Audiology & Hearing Center, we offer safe and effective ear cleaning to remove blockages and relieve pressure without damaging the ear canal.

Medications for Infections

If an infection is detected, we can coordinate with your primary care doctor or ENT to prescribe the appropriate antibiotics or decongestants.

Hearing Aids or Assistive Devices

If your ear pressure is linked to a chronic condition like Meniere’s, hearing aids can improve your quality of life and help manage related symptoms. Audiology & Hearing Center offers a wide selection of hearing aids in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL.

Surgical Referrals

In more serious cases, such as fluid that doesn’t drain properly or recurring infections, we may refer you to an ENT for surgical options like placing tubes in the middle ear.

When to Seek Immediate Help

While ear pressure is often mild, certain red flag symptoms mean you should seek care immediately:

  • Sudden or severe ear pain
  • Rapid hearing loss
  • Dizziness or balance problems
  • Tinnitus (ringing or buzzing in the ears)
  • Discharge from the ear

In these cases, an audiologist is a great first stop for a fast and accurate evaluation. Early intervention is key to preventing long-term issues.

How to Prevent Ear Pressure

Avoid Using Cotton Swabs

Cotton swabs can push ear wax deeper and cause impaction. Use ear-safe drops or visit your audiologist for regular cleanings instead.

Protect Ears During Altitude Changes

Swallowing, yawning, or chewing gum during flights or elevation changes can help equalize pressure in the middle ear.

Manage Allergies and Sinus Issues

Treating sinus congestion or allergies can keep the Eustachian tubes open and prevent clogged ears and pressure buildup.

Audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL

If you’re experiencing pressure in your ears, don’t wait and wonder—get expert help. At Audiology & Hearing Center, our skilled team offers compassionate care, advanced diagnostics, and proven treatment options tailored just for you.

Our audiologists in Tampa Bay and Venice are equipped to address your ear discomfort, ear pressure, and hearing needs.

Call us today or fill out our online form to schedule your consultation or hearing test. Your ears deserve expert care—let’s make sure they get it.

Our clinics are located in Tampa Palms, FL, Tampa Westchase, FL, Palm Harbor, FL, and Venice, FL.

data.meta_title: Real Ear Measurement | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Audiology & Hearing Center has audiologists in Tampa Bay & Venice, FL providing real ear measurements as part of hearing aid fitting best practices.
data.content:
Audiology & Hearing Centers has audiologists in Tampa Palms, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase FL and Alpharetta, GA providing real ear measurements as part of hearing aid fitting best practices.

Real Ear Measurement (REM) is a special test used to check how well a hearing aid is working inside a person’s ear. Everyone’s ears are different, and sound can change as it travels into the ear canal. REM helps audiologists make sure the hearing aid is adjusted just right for each person’s needs.

Why Is Real Ear Measurement Important?

REM makes sure hearing aids provide the right level of sound for each person. Without this test, the settings may not be accurate, which could make sounds too loud, too soft, or unclear. By using REM, audiologists can give patients the best hearing experience possible.

How Does Real Ear Measurement Work?

During REM, a small microphone is placed inside the ear along with the hearing aid. This microphone measures how sound moves inside the ear and helps the audiologist adjust the hearing aid so speech and other sounds are clear and comfortable.

The History of Real Ear Measurement

Real Ear Measurement has been used in audiology for decades. Before this method, audiologists relied on general hearing aid settings that did not always work well for everyone. In the 1980s, REM became widely used to customize hearing aids for each person. Today, it is considered the gold standard in hearing aid fitting.

Why Perform Real Ear Measurements?

Real Ear Measurement is one of the most important steps in a hearing aid fitting. Here’s why:

  • Ensures Accuracy – Everyone’s ears are shaped differently, and sound reacts in unique ways inside each ear. REM helps adjust the hearing aid based on real measurements, not just guesses.
  • Verifies Hearing Aid Performance – Many hearing aids come with settings based on average ear shapes, but these may not be right for you. REM fine-tunes the settings for the best results.
  • Better Speech Clarity – By measuring and adjusting sounds inside the ear, REM makes sure speech is clear and easy to understand.
  • Prevents Sounds From Being Too Loud or Too Soft – Without REM, a hearing aid might not give the right level of amplification, making conversations difficult.
  • Reduces Follow-Up Visits – When hearing aids are fitted using REM, fewer adjustments are needed later, saving time and hassle.

Since REM checks the hearing aid while it’s in the actual ear, it provides the most accurate and personalized fit possible. That’s why audiologists consider it the gold standard in hearing aid fittings.

How Does Real Ear Measurement Work?

The process of Real Ear Measurement involves a few simple steps:

  1. Placing the Probe Microphone – A tiny microphone is placed inside the ear canal, near the eardrum.
  2. Testing Sounds – The audiologist plays speech sounds and other noises through a speaker to see how they change inside the ear.
  3. Adjusting the Hearing Aid – The audiologist compares the results to a target range and adjusts the hearing aid so it provides the right amount of amplification.
  4. Final Check – Once the adjustments are made, the audiologist re-tests the settings to ensure the best possible hearing experience.

Calibration of the Probe Microphone

Before testing, the audiologist calibrates the probe microphone to ensure it is working correctly. This step helps make sure the measurements are accurate and reliable.

Are Real Ear Measurements Necessary?

Yes! REM is an essential part of hearing aid fitting. Here’s why:

  • Without REM, hearing aids may not provide the right level of sound for a person’s hearing loss.
  • Some over-the-counter (OTC) hearing aids do not include REM, which means their settings are not customized to the user’s ears.
  • Many people who struggle with hearing aids often haven’t had REM done to fine-tune their devices.
  • Studies show that people who receive REM have better satisfaction with their hearing aids.

If you want the best hearing experience, REM should always be part of your hearing aid fitting.

Real Ear Measurement vs. Automatic Hearing Aid Programming

Many hearing aids today come with auto programming or a manufacturer’s first fit setting. But there’s a big difference between this and Real Ear Measurement.

  • Auto programming adjusts the hearing aid based on general ear shapes, not your actual ear.
  • REM measures sound inside your own ear and adjusts the hearing aid for your unique hearing needs.
  • Manufacturer’s First Fit is just a starting point, while REM ensures a perfect fit.

While automatic programming is helpful, it does not replace Real Ear Measurement. If you want the most accurate and comfortable hearing aid settings, REM is a must.

Audiology & Hearing Centers has audiologists providing real ear measurements as part of hearing aid fitting best practices.

Real Ear Measurement and Hearing Aid Success

Real Ear Measurement helps people get the best results from their hearing aids. It ensures that speech is clear, sounds are comfortable, and the hearing aid is working just right for the user.

Hearing Aid Fittings in Tampa Bay, FL

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we use Real Ear Measurement to give our patients the best hearing experience possible. If you or a loved one are getting hearing aids, make sure REM is part of the process.

Call us today to schedule an appointment!

Our clinics are located in Tampa Palms, FL, Tampa Westchase, FL, Palm Harbor, FL, and Alpharetta, GA.
