data.meta_title: Sensorineural Hearing Loss Treatment in Tampa Bay & Venice, FL | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Audiology & Hearing Center has audiologists providing sensorineural hearing loss treatment in Tampa Bay & Venice, FL.
data.content:
Audiology & Hearing Centers has audiologists providing sensorineural hearing loss treatment in Tampa Palms, Venice, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Sarasota, Bonita Springs, Naples, Ocala FL and Alpharetta, GA.

Sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) happens when the inner ear or the nerve that connects the ear to the brain is damaged. This type of hearing loss makes it hard to hear soft sounds and can make loud sounds seem muffled or unclear. Unlike other types of hearing loss, SNHL is usually permanent because the tiny hair cells in the inner ear do not grow back once they are damaged.

Why Seeking Treatment Is Important

If left untreated, SNHL can make it harder to understand speech, enjoy conversations, and stay connected with loved ones. It can also lead to isolation, frustration, and even memory problems over time. Getting the right treatment can help improve hearing, communication, and overall quality of life.

Symptoms of Sensorineural Hearing Loss

SNHL can develop suddenly or slowly over time. Some common signs include:

  • Difficulty hearing soft sounds
  • Trouble understanding speech—people may sound like they are mumbling
  • Asking others to repeat themselves often
  • Struggling to hear in noisy places like restaurants or crowds
  • Ringing in the ears (tinnitus)
  • Hearing better in one ear than the other
  • Feeling like sounds are muffled or distant
  • Difficulty hearing high-pitched sounds like children’s voices or birds chirping

If you or a loved one experience any of these symptoms, it’s important to get a hearing test as soon as possible. Early diagnosis and treatment can make a big difference in how well you hear and communicate.

Causes of Sensorineural Hearing Loss

SNHL can happen for many reasons. Some of the most common causes include:

  • Aging (Presbycusis) – The most common cause of SNHL is natural aging. Over time, the tiny hair cells in the inner ear wear down and stop working properly.
  • Loud Noise Exposure – Repeated exposure to loud noises, like concerts, fireworks, or power tools, can damage the inner ear.
  • Genetics – Some people are born with SNHL or have a family history of hearing loss.
  • Certain Medications – Some drugs, called ototoxic medications, can harm the inner ear and cause hearing loss.
  • Head Injuries – A severe head trauma can damage the inner ear or the hearing nerve.
  • Illnesses and Infections – Viruses, high fevers, and autoimmune diseases can sometimes cause permanent hearing loss.

Understanding the cause of your SNHL can help your audiologist recommend the best treatment.

Sensorineural Hearing Loss Diagnosis and Treatment

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, diagnosing SNHL starts with a hearing test. This test measures how well you hear different sounds and speech. If hearing loss is found, we will discuss the best treatment options to help you hear better.

What Is the Best Treatment for Sensorineural Hearing Loss?

The best treatment for SNHL depends on how much hearing loss you have. For mild to moderate cases, hearing aids are usually the best choice. If hearing loss is severe and hearing aids don’t help enough, cochlear implants may be recommended. For those with single-sided deafness or conductive hearing loss combined with SNHL, a bone-anchored hearing aid may be the best solution.

Your audiologist will discuss the best treatment plan based on your specific hearing needs.

Can You Fix Sensorineural Hearing Loss?

Currently, sensorineural hearing loss cannot be reversed because the tiny hair cells in the inner ear do not grow back once damaged. However, treatments like hearing aids and cochlear implants can help restore hearing ability and improve quality of life. The sooner you seek help, the better your chances of adapting to these treatments and staying connected with the world around you.

How Do You Prevent Further Sensorineural Hearing Loss?

While SNHL cannot always be prevented, there are steps you can take to protect your hearing:

  • Avoid loud noises – Wear ear protection when around loud sounds, like concerts, machinery, or fireworks.
  • Turn down the volume – Keep your music and TV at a safe listening level.
  • Take listening breaks – If you use headphones or earbuds, give your ears a break every hour.
  • Check your medications – Ask your doctor if any medicines you take can affect hearing.
  • Get regular hearing check-ups – Early detection can help slow down hearing loss and improve treatment options.

Taking care of your ears today can help protect your hearing for the future.

data.content:
Hearing loss can make phone conversations difficult and frustrating. You might find yourself missing important words or struggling to keep up with the conversation. At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we’re here to help. That’s why we offer CapTel—a captioned telephone service that makes it easier to communicate by showing written captions of phone conversations in real time.

What is CapTel?

CapTel stands for Captioned Telephone. It’s a special phone designed for people with hearing loss who need extra support during phone calls. CapTel phones display real-time captions of everything the caller says, so you can listen and read along. It’s like having subtitles for your phone conversations—helping you stay connected and confident.

How CapTel Works

CapTel phones come with a screen that shows captions as the other person speaks. These captions are provided by a secure captioning service that transcribes speech in real time. Depending on the model, CapTel phones work with landlines, internet connections, or mobile networks. Some models even include amplified sound for added clarity.

CapTel Phone Models

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we can help you choose the right CapTel phone for your needs. Here are the most popular models available:

CapTel 2400i

  • Large, touchscreen display
  • Easy-to-read captions and keypad
  • Amplified sound and Bluetooth compatibility
  • Requires high-speed internet and a landline

CapTel 840i

  • Traditional-style phone with large screen
  • Amplified handset with adjustable volume
  • Captions delivered via internet
  • Ideal for users who prefer a standard phone layout

CapTel 880i

  • Designed for individuals with low vision
  • Extra-large screen and large-font captions
  • High-contrast display and built-in amplification
  • Requires internet and landline

CapTel 840

  • Perfect for homes without internet access
  • Captions provided through a traditional phone line
  • Easy-to-use display and buttons

CapTel Mobile App

  • Get captioning on the go with your smartphone or tablet
  • Available for both iOS and Android devices
  • Use captions anywhere—whether at home or away

Benefits of Using CapTel

  • See and hear every word during phone calls
  • Stay in touch with family, friends, and healthcare providers
  • Easy-to-use technology—no technical skills required
  • Free phone and captioning service for eligible users
  • Mobile app available for convenience wherever you go

Who Qualifies for CapTel?

CapTel is available to people with hearing loss that makes it difficult to understand phone conversations.

  • A certified audiologist must confirm your hearing loss
  • There is no age limit—CapTel phones are available to qualified individuals of all ages
  • Our team at Audiology & Hearing Centers can guide you through the process

How to Get a CapTel Phone

Getting a CapTel phone is simple:

  1. Schedule a hearing evaluation at our Tampa Bay or Venice clinic
  2. Our audiologists will confirm your hearing loss and complete the necessary certification
  3. CapTel will provide the phone, installation, and training at no cost
  4. Start enjoying clearer phone conversations at home or on the go

CapTel phones are an excellent companion to your hearing aids or cochlear implants. The amplified sound and visual captions offer an extra level of support, helping you hear more clearly and communicate more confidently.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is CapTel really free?

Yes. For those who qualify, the phone and captioning service are provided at no cost, thanks to federal assistance programs.

Do I need internet access for CapTel?

Some models (like the CapTel 2400i and 840i) require internet, while others (like the CapTel 840) work without it.

Can I use CapTel if I already wear hearing aids?

Absolutely. CapTel works very well with hearing aids and helps make conversations even clearer.

Free installation and training are included with every CapTel phone, so you’ll have support from day one.

data.meta_title: Swimmer’s Ear | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Learn the causes, symptoms, and treatment options for swimmer’s ear from the experts at Audiology & Hearing Center in Tampa Bay and Venice, FL. Protect your hearing with professional care.
data.content:
Swimmer’s ear, or otitis externa, is an outer ear canal infection that commonly occurs when water becomes trapped in the ear. This moist environment can allow bacteria or fungi to grow and cause inflammation. While swimming is often to blame, anyone exposed to excess moisture, injury, or irritation in the ear canal can develop this condition.

At Audiology & Hearing Center, our hearing care professionals are trained to recognize early signs of ear issues like swimmer’s ear and can guide you to the appropriate care. 

Don’t let ear pain linger. Early diagnosis and treatment can make a big difference.

What Causes Swimmer’s Ear?

Swimmer’s ear develops when your ear’s natural defenses are compromised. Normally, the ear canal’s slight acidity, cerumen (earwax), and structure help fend off infection. But when that balance is disrupted, infection can follow. Common causes include:

  • Water trapped after swimming, bathing, or humid weather
  • Allergic reactions to hair sprays, dyes, or earrings
  • Damage from cotton swabs or fingernails
  • Irritation from hearing aids, earbuds, or earplugs

Your Ear’s Natural Defenses

Your ears are naturally equipped to protect themselves. Earwax helps:

  • Trap dust and bacteria
  • Maintain a healthy, slightly acidic environment
  • Repel water

The curved shape of your ear canal also discourages deep intrusion of water or foreign objects. When overcleaning or frequent exposure to moisture upsets these defenses, infection is more likely.

However, too much ear wax can also be detrimental to your hearing health. Audio Help Hearing Center provides professional ear wax removal in Ocala, FL, Tampa Palms, FL, Tampa Westchase, FL, Palm Harbor, FL, and Venice, FL, and other key locations.

Why Do People Get Swimmer’s Ear?

Swimmer’s ear typically arises from a combination of moisture and irritation. You may be at higher risk if you:

  • Swim or are exposed to water frequently (especially in warm or poorly maintained water)
  • Use cotton swabs or insert objects into your ears
  • Have sensitive skin or eczema
  • Use hearing devices that cause friction

What Are the Symptoms of Swimmer’s Ear?

Mild symptoms:

  • Itching or slight discomfort
  • Redness in the ear canal
  • Clear fluid drainage

Moderate symptoms:

  • Increased pain, especially when touching the ear
  • Swelling and more noticeable redness
  • Cloudy or yellowish drainage

Severe symptoms:

  • Intense pain that spreads to the neck or face
  • Swollen lymph nodes
  • Fever or complete ear blockage

How Is Swimmer’s Ear Diagnosed?

An audiologist or healthcare provider can diagnose swimmer’s ear by examining the ear with an otoscope. Pain when the outer ear is gently pulled may signal infection. In some cases, fluid may be tested to identify the cause, especially if symptoms persist despite treatment.

How Is Swimmer’s Ear Treated?

Most cases are easily treatable. Your healthcare provider may:

  • Prescribe antibiotic ear drops
  • Recommend steroid drops to reduce inflammation
  • Suggest pain relievers

If the ear is swollen shut, a soft wick may be placed in the canal to help the drops reach the infection. Keeping the ear dry and avoiding further irritation is key. Severe cases might require oral antibiotics or referral to a specialist.

Can Swimmer’s Ear Be Prevented?

Yes, swimmers’ ear can be prevented! Prevention tips include:

  • Dry your ears thoroughly after swimming or bathing
  • Use waterproof earplugs or a swimcap
  • Avoid inserting anything into your ears
  • Be mindful of allergens or chemicals near the ears
  • Avoid swimming after getting an ear infection or if you recently had ear injuries

Risk Factors for Swimmer’s Ear

You may be more susceptible to swimmer’s ear if you:

  • Swim often
  • Wear hearing aids or earbuds regularly
  • Clean your ears aggressively
  • Have narrow ear canals or skin conditions
  • Have a history of ear infections

Potential Complications

If untreated, swimmer’s ear can lead to:

  • Temporary hearing loss due to swelling or fluid
  • Chronic otitis externa (persistent or recurring infections)
  • Cellulitis (skin infection spreading to surrounding areas)
  • Malignant otitis externa, a rare but serious condition affecting the skull base (especially in diabetics)
  • Infection spreading to deeper tissues or nerves in very rare cases

Prompt care is the best way to avoid complications.

Conclusion

Swimmer’s ear may begin with a small itch, but it can quickly escalate into something much more serious. If you’re experiencing discomfort, discharge, or muffled hearing, don’t ignore it. 

Audiology & Hearing Center is here to help you protect your hearing and feel your best.

data.meta_title: Tinnitus Support | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Audiology & Hearing Center provides expert tinnitus support in Tampa Bay & Venice, FL.
data.content:
Audiology & Hearing Centers provides expert tinnitus evaluations and support in Tampa Palms, Venice, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Bonita Springs, Ocala FL.

Tinnitus is the ringing, buzzing, hissing, or clicking sounds you hear in your ears when there is no outside noise causing it. It can be mild and occasional or constant and loud, making it hard to focus, sleep, or enjoy daily life.

What Causes Tinnitus?

Tinnitus can happen for many reasons, including:

  • Hearing loss – The brain may create sounds to make up for missing sound signals.
  • Loud noise exposure – Listening to loud music, working around machinery, or attending concerts can damage hearing.
  • Ear wax buildup – Too much ear wax can block the ear and cause ringing.
  • Medications – Some medicines have tinnitus as a side effect.
  • Health conditions – Issues like high blood pressure, ear infections, or TMJ problems can contribute to tinnitus.

Tinnitus Evaluations

If you hear ringing or buzzing in your ears, an audiologist can evaluate your tinnitus to find out what might be causing it. During the visit, the audiologist may:

  • Ask about your symptoms – When did the tinnitus start? How often do you hear it?
  • Check your ears – A physical exam can look for blockages or infections.
  • Perform a hearing test – Hearing loss is a common cause of tinnitus.
  • Measure your tinnitus – You may be asked to match the sound of your tinnitus to different tones and volumes.

Tinnitus Care Options

While there is no cure for tinnitus, many treatments can help reduce symptoms and make it easier to live with.

Sound Therapy

Using background noise to mask the ringing can help your brain tune it out. Options include:

  • White noise machines
  • Fans or soft music
  • Hearing aids with sound masking features

Hearing Aids

If you have hearing loss, hearing aids can improve overall sound clarity, making tinnitus less noticeable. Some models also have built-in tinnitus masking to reduce the ringing.

Counseling and Stress Management

Tinnitus can be stressful and frustrating. Talking to a professional or using relaxation techniques like meditation, deep breathing, and exercise can help manage symptoms.

Lifestyle Changes

Certain habits can make tinnitus worse. Try to:

  • Limit caffeine and alcohol
  • Avoid loud noises or use ear protection
  • Reduce stress through relaxation exercises
  • Get good sleep to help your brain adjust to the ringing

Get good sleep to help your brain adjust to the ringing

Frequently Asked Questions About Tinnitus

Can tinnitus go away on its own?

Tinnitus sometimes goes away on its own, especially if it’s caused by temporary factors like loud noise exposure or ear wax buildup. However, if it lasts for weeks or gets worse, it’s best to see an audiologist for an evaluation.

Can stress make tinnitus worse?

Yes, stress and anxiety can make tinnitus seem louder. When the brain is under stress, it becomes more aware of internal sounds, including ringing in the ears. Managing stress through relaxation techniques can help reduce symptoms.

Is tinnitus a sign of hearing loss?

Tinnitus is often linked to hearing loss, but not always. Some people with normal hearing still experience tinnitus, while others with hearing loss may not. A hearing test can determine if hearing loss is a factor.

Can hearing aids really help with tinnitus?

Yes, hearing aids can help by amplifying external sounds, making tinnitus less noticeable. Many modern hearing aids also have tinnitus masking features that use soothing sounds to help distract from the ringing.

Can ear wax cause tinnitus?

Yes, too much ear wax can block the ear canal and create a ringing or buzzing sound. If ear wax buildup is the cause, professional ear wax removal can provide relief. Audiology & Hearing Centers provides professional ear wax removal in Palm Harbor, FL, and nearby locations.

Tinnitus Care in Tampa Palms, Venice, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, and Bonita Springs FL.

If tinnitus is affecting your daily life, you don’t have to suffer in silence. 

Our Audiologists and Hearing Aid Specialists are here to help with expert tinnitus care options in Tampa Palms, Venice, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, and Bonita Springs FL.

Call us today to schedule your tinnitus evaluation and take the first step toward relief!

Unitron is a Canadian company that has been making hearing aids for over 50 years. Unitron hearing aids are designed to help people hear better in many situations. They provide clear sound, easy-to-use features, and a comfortable fit.

Current Unitron Hearing Aid Models

These are the latest Unitron hearing aids available at the Audiology & Hearing Centers.

Unitron Insera S-R

Powered by the advanced Smile platform, the Insera S-R is meticulously crafted to fit the unique contours of your ear, offering a discreet design without compromising on power. Whether you’re in a crowded restaurant or a quiet home, its AI-driven technology automatically adjusts to your environment, making hearing feel natural and effortless. 

It comes with robust Bluetooth connectivity and all-day battery life to help you stay connected to the people and devices that matter most.

Unique Perks and Features:

  • EarMatch™ Customization: An evolved design process that uses deep-fit modeling and intentional microphone placement to optimize sound performance specifically for your unique ear anatomy.
  • Universal Bluetooth® Connectivity: Enjoy stable, hands-free calls and high-quality media streaming. The Insera S-R features up to 6x more wireless transmission power and double the connection range of previous models.
  • Rechargeable Custom Fit: Combines the “invisible” appeal of a custom-molded hearing aid with the modern convenience of lithium-ion reliability and a magnetic “snap-and-charge” docking station.
  • HyperFocus & Speech Enhancement: Advanced spatial features that zoom in on speech during loud conversations while reducing the listening effort required in quieter settings to prevent “listening fatigue.”

IP68-Rated Durability: Engineered for everyday life with proven wax protection and a rugged, water-resistant design that ensures dependable performance in all conditions.

Unitron Moxi S-R

The Unitron Moxi S-R is a small, stylish hearing aid that’s made to fit comfortably and work reliably every day. It’s powered by Unitron’s newest Smile platform, which helps you hear clearly in more places and enjoy life’s sounds with ease. 

Moxi S-R is also built to handle water and moisture, so you don’t have to worry about splashes or weather. With its easy-to-use charger and a portable ChargerGo, you can keep your hearing aids powered, whether you’re at home or on the move.

Unitron Moxi V-RS

The Unitron Moxi V-RS is a small, lightweight hearing aid that sits behind the ear. It is designed to be almost invisible while delivering clear sound in both quiet and noisy places.

✔ Bluetooth Connection: Connect to your phone, TV, or other devices to hear calls, music, and videos directly in your hearing aids.
✔ Rechargeable Batteries: No need to change small batteries—just place them in a charger.
✔ App Help: Use the Remote Plus App to adjust volume and settings. Your hearing provider can also fine-tune them remotely.

Unitron Moxi Vivante (V)

✔ Rechargeable Battery: Provides all-day power after a single charge.

  • In-the-Canal (ITC)
  • Completely-in-the-Canal (CIC)

Assistive Listening Devices & Technology by Unitron

data.meta_title: Walmart Hearing Aids | Audiology & Hearing Center
data.meta_description: Thinking about buying hearing aids from Walmart? Learn the pros, cons, and why seeing an audiologist offers better results for your hearing health.
data.content:
Hearing loss is a personal journey, and finding the right hearing aid is an important decision. With more retailers offering over-the-counter hearing devices, including Walmart, it’s easy to be drawn to low prices and convenience. Walmart offers a wide range of hearing aids and sound amplifiers both in-store and online, but are they the right solution for you?

While some devices available at Walmart may offer short-term help for mild hearing challenges, not all products are created equal. Hearing health is complex and often requires professional evaluation, fitting, and follow-up. Audiologists are trained to identify the type and severity of hearing loss and provide personalized care that a retail store simply can’t offer.

In this guide, we’ll look at the hearing devices offered at Walmart, explain the differences between medical-grade hearing aids and cheaper alternatives, and help you decide what’s truly best for your hearing health.

A Look at Walmart’s Hearing Devices: Understanding the Difference

Walmart has been selling hearing devices for years, offering a mix of personal sound amplifiers and, more recently, FDA-registered over-the-counter (OTC) hearing aids. While this has made hearing support more accessible, it’s also created confusion for many consumers.

What’s the difference between real hearing aids and sound amplifiers?

Real hearing aids are medical devices approved by the FDA. They are designed to treat hearing loss by processing and amplifying specific sound frequencies based on your hearing needs. They require careful fitting and adjustment, often performed by an audiologist.

Personal Sound Amplification Products (PSAPs), on the other hand, simply amplify all sound equally—much like turning up the volume on a speaker. They’re not designed to treat hearing loss and can actually cause more harm than good if used improperly.

How to tell the difference:

  • Check for FDA Registration: Legitimate OTC hearing aids are listed with the FDA.
  • Read the packaging: Words like “medical device” or “hearing aid” suggest more legitimacy than terms like “amplifier.”
  • Watch the price: If it seems too good to be true (e.g., $50–$100), it probably isn’t a real hearing aid.
  • Look for support: Reputable hearing aid brands offer customer support, warranties, and professional guidance.

Walmart’s Best-Selling Hearing Aids

Walmart offers a variety of hearing devices, both online and in some retail locations. Here’s a quick overview of some of the most popular products:

HearingAssist Rechargeable OTC Hearing Aids

These devices are marketed as OTC hearing aids and are rechargeable, making them attractive for new users. However, they have limited customization options.

Go Hearing Go Lite and Go Prime

These small, discreet devices offer basic sound amplification and are easy to use. They may be suitable for someone with very mild hearing difficulty, but don’t offer features like directional microphones or background noise reduction.

MDHearingAid AIR

This brand claims to offer “medical-grade” hearing aids without the need for a hearing test. While affordable, they don’t provide a custom fit and may not be effective for all types of hearing loss.

Lucid Hearing Engage

Sold at select Walmart locations, these hearing aids come with a smartphone app and are FDA-registered. However, without professional guidance, users may struggle to adjust the settings to their needs.

While these products offer convenience, they lack the custom fitting and in-person support that make a real difference when it comes to hearing success.

Are Walmart Hearing Aids Any Good?

Walmart hearing aids can be a decent starting point for people with mild hearing concerns, but they’re not a substitute for professional hearing care.

Pros:

  • Affordability: Prices range from $200–$1,000, much lower than traditional hearing aids.
  • Accessibility: Available without a prescription, often with easy online ordering.
  • Rechargeable options: Many models now come with rechargeable batteries.

Cons:

  • No professional fitting: Hearing aids work best when they’re tailored to your hearing loss.
  • Limited features: Most lack advanced features like feedback suppression, tinnitus masking, or Bluetooth.
  • No follow-up care: Adjustments, maintenance, and troubleshooting are left to the user.

Why an Audiologist Makes a Difference

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we provide more than just hearing aids—we provide personalized care. We start with a hearing test, walk you through the results, and help you choose a device that fits your lifestyle. We also offer warranties, repairs, and follow-up appointments to ensure long-term success.

Contact us to be connected to expert audiologists and hearing aids in Tampa Bay, FL and Venice, FL.

Do Cheap Hearing Aids Work?

As audiologists, we understand the appeal of cheap hearing aids—but we also see the limitations.

Low-cost hearing devices often amplify all sounds, including background noise, which can make conversations even harder to understand. They may not filter out unwanted sounds or adjust automatically in noisy settings.

We’ve had many patients come in frustrated after trying cheap alternatives. While they might help in a quiet room, they don’t support real-world listening needs like a properly programmed hearing aid can.

Spending less upfront can cost more in the long run if the device doesn’t help—or worse, causes further hearing damage from improper use.

Do More Expensive Hearing Aids Work Better?

In many cases, yes. More expensive hearing aids offer more features, including:

  • Custom sound programming
  • Bluetooth streaming
  • Tinnitus support
  • Automatic environmental adjustments
  • Better noise reduction and speech clarity

But it’s not just the technology that makes a difference—it’s the professional service that comes with it. Our team offers:

  • Hearing evaluations
  • Custom fittings
  • Ongoing care and support
  • Access to top brands backed by manufacturer warranties

With the right hearing aid and the right support, you’re not just buying a device—you’re investing in better hearing and a better quality of life.

Your Hearing Deserves More Than a One-Size-Fits-All Solution

Walmart hearing aids may seem like a quick fix, but they’re rarely the best solution for long-term hearing health. A proper hearing evaluation and professional care can make all the difference.

Audiology & Hearing Centers connects you to expert audiologists and hearing aids in Tampa Bay, FL and Venice, FL.

If you’re ready to hear more clearly and confidently, schedule a hearing test with our team today. We’re here to help you find the hearing solution that truly fits your needs.

Our clinics are located in Tampa Palms, FL, Tampa Westchase, FL, Palm Harbor, FL, Venice, FL, Sarasota, FL, and Alpharetta, GA.

data.meta_title: Why Do Ears Produce So Much Wax? | Audiology and Hearing Centers
data.meta_description: Wondering why your ears make so much wax? Learn what’s normal, when to worry, and when to see an audiologist in Palm Harbor, FL. Call today for help.
data.content:
• Earwax is normal and helps protect, clean, and moisturize your ears, even when it feels like you have “too much.”
  • Some people make more earwax because of age, genetics, health, or habits like using cotton swabs.
  • If earwax causes fullness, pain, or hearing loss, it’s time to see an audiologist in Palm Harbor, FL for safe, professional care.

Why Do Ears Produce So Much Wax?

If you feel like you are always dealing with earwax, you may wonder, “Why do ears produce so much wax?” You are not alone. Many people come into Audiology & Hearing Centers with the same question and a lot of worry.

The good news is that earwax, also called cerumen, is normal. Your body makes it on purpose, and it actually plays a very important role. Still, it can be frustrating when it seems like there is more wax than there should be, or when it starts to block hearing. In this simple guide, we will explain why your ears make wax, why some people make more than others, and when it is time to see an audiologist for professional ear wax removal.

What Is Earwax, Really?

Earwax is not dirt. It is made from tiny glands in the skin of your ear canal. These glands mix oil, sweat, dead skin cells, and tiny bits of dust into a sticky, waxy substance. That substance is earwax.

Your ears are smart. As you talk, chew, and move your jaw, the skin in your ear canal slowly moves old wax outward. This is your ear’s natural cleaning system. Most of the time, you do not need to do anything at all.

Why Your Body Makes Earwax

Earwax has three main jobs:

  • Protect: Earwax traps dust, germs, and small particles so they do not reach your eardrum.
  • Clean: As wax moves out of your ear, it carries dirt and dead skin with it.
  • Moisturize: Earwax keeps the skin in your ear canal from getting dry and itchy.

So, even if it feels like a problem sometimes, wax is actually your ear’s built-in shield.

Why Do Some People Have “Too Much” Earwax?

If earwax is normal, why do ears produce so much wax in some people? There are several reasons.

1. Genetics and Body Type

Just like hair color or eye shape, earwax can be influenced by your genes. Some people’s bodies simply produce more wax. Others may have thicker or stickier wax. A narrow or twisty ear canal can also make it harder for wax to move out on its own, so it builds up.

2. Age

As we get older, the glands in the ear canal can change. Earwax can become drier and harder. Older adults may also have more hair in the ear canal. This can trap wax, making it harder for the ear to clear itself, so it seems like there is more than before.

3. Using Cotton Swabs and Other Objects

This one surprises a lot of people. Many people use cotton swabs because they want clean ears. But cotton swabs often push wax deeper into the ear canal instead of pulling it out. Over time, this can lead to a plug of wax that feels like “too much.”

Other objects, like hairpins, pens, or earbuds, can do the same thing. They can pack wax in, scratch the delicate skin, and even cause infections.

4. Hearing Aids, Earbuds, and Earplugs

If you wear hearing aids, earbuds, or earplugs often, you may notice more wax problems. These devices block the natural movement of wax toward the outside of the ear. They can also push wax back in each time you insert them.

This does not mean you should stop using your hearing aids or earbuds. It does mean you should have your ears checked regularly by a hearing care professional, especially if you live in or near Palm Harbor, FL.

5. Skin and Health Conditions

Certain skin or health issues can also change how much wax your ears make. Conditions like eczema, psoriasis, or infections can affect the skin in the ear canal. When the skin is dry or irritated, your body may respond by making more wax.

Signs You May Have Too Much Earwax

Sometimes extra earwax does not cause any problems at all. But if it builds up and creates a blockage, you may notice:

  • A feeling of fullness or pressure in the ear
  • Sounds seeming muffled or softer
  • Ringing in the ear (tinnitus)
  • Earache or discomfort
  • Itching or odor from the ear

If you notice these signs, it is important not to dig into your ear. Instead, contact an audiologist or hearing care provider.

Audiology & Hearing Centers has audiologists in Palm Harbor, FL and other key locations in Florida and Georgia providing expert hearing care such as ear wax removal, hearing aid fittings, etc.

Why You Should Not Try to “Dig Out” Earwax

It is very tempting to grab a cotton swab when your ear feels blocked. But digging into the ear canal can cause more harm than good.

Here’s why:

  • You can push the wax deeper, creating a hard plug.
  • You can scratch the skin, which can lead to infection.
  • You might even damage the eardrum if you go too far.

Over-the-counter ear candles are also unsafe and not recommended. They do not truly pull wax out, and they can cause burns and other injuries.

If you feel like your ears produce too much wax, the safest step is to ask a professional for help.

How Audiologists Safely Remove Earwax

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we see many people who feel like they have “too much earwax.” We understand that it can be uncomfortable and even scary. Our goal is to make the process safe, gentle, and easy to understand.

Common methods we may use include:

Gentle Tools

An audiologist may use special instruments, like a small loop or suction device, to carefully remove wax while looking in your ear with a light. This is quick and usually painless.

Softening Drops and Irrigation

Sometimes the wax is very dry or hard. In these cases, softening drops may be used first. Then warm water might be gently flushed into the ear canal to rinse the wax out. This is done in a controlled way with the right tools and training.

The method used will depend on your ears, your health history, and how much wax is present.

When Earwax Becomes a Bigger Problem

Most of the time, earwax is just a minor issue. But sometimes it can point to something more. That is another reason to let a hearing care professional check your ears rather than trying to handle it alone.

You should contact an audiologist or doctor right away if you have:

  • Sudden hearing loss
  • Strong pain in one or both ears
  • Drainage that looks like pus or blood
  • Dizziness or balance problems
  • A feeling that something is “really wrong”

These can be signs of infection or other ear problems that need medical care right away.

How to Help Your Ears Stay Healthy

You cannot change your genes, and you may not be able to fully control how much wax your ears produce. But you can support your ear health with a few simple habits:

1. Stop Using Cotton Swabs in the Ear Canal

You can use a washcloth to gently wipe the outer ear, but do not place cotton swabs or other objects inside. Remember: nothing smaller than your elbow should go in your ear.

2. Give Your Ears a Break from Devices

If you wear earbuds or earplugs a lot, try to give your ears some “air time” when possible. If you wear hearing aids, ask your provider how often you should have your ears checked for wax.

3. Follow Your Audiologist’s Advice

If you have a history of heavy wax buildup, your audiologist may suggest a schedule for regular ear checks or cleanings. This can keep wax from building up and causing bigger problems later.

When to See an Audiologist

You do not have to wait until your ear feels completely blocked. It is a good idea to see an audiologist if:

  • You feel like your ears produce more wax than they used to
  • You have had earwax blockages in the past
  • You wear hearing aids and notice more feedback or muffled sound
  • You are not sure if your ear is plugged with wax or something else

At Audiology & Hearing Centers, we can check your ears, safely remove wax if needed, and test your hearing to make sure everything is working as it should.

We also take time to answer your questions. We know that hearing and ear health can feel confusing, and we want you to feel informed and comfortable.

Why Do Ears Produce So Much Wax? The Bottom Line

So, why do ears produce so much wax in some people? The answer is that your body is trying to protect your ears. Earwax is part of your ear’s natural defense system. Some people simply make more wax, or their ears have a harder time clearing it out. Age, genetics, earbuds, hearing aids, and health conditions can all play a part.

While earwax is normal, you should not ignore symptoms like fullness, pain, or hearing changes. Instead of trying to fix it yourself, let a professional help.

If you feel like your ears produce too much wax, we invite you to contact Audiology & Hearing Centers.

We are here to answer your questions, check your ears, and help you hear and feel your best.

data.content:
Audiology & Hearing Centers is an authorized provider of Widex hearing aids in Tampa Palms, Venice, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Sarasota, Bonita Springs FL and Alpharetta, GA.

Widex is a leader in using artificial intelligence (AI) to make better hearing aids. They know that to get the best sound, human and artificial intelligence must work together. Over the years, they have designed hearing aids that give clear, natural sound. 

Widex Hearing Aid Models in Tampa Palms, Venice, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Sarasota, Bonita Springs FL and Alpharetta, GA

Here are the Widex hearing aids available at Audiology & Hearing Centers.

Widex Allure

The Widex Allure is a sleek, modern hearing aid designed to make everyday sounds feel effortless and natural. With its latest technology and lightning-fast sound processing, Allure helps you stay connected to the conversations and moments that matter most.

This newest hearing aid from Widex adjusts in real time to deliver clear, comfortable sound. It’s packed with smart features like feedback control to reduce whistling and speech enhancement to help you hear voices more clearly, even in busy places.

Key Features:

  • Speech Enhancer Pro: Uses a 52-band high-resolution engine to sharpen speech clarity, even in noisy environments.
  • Dynamic Feedback Controller: Continuously adapts to reduce whistling and unwanted feedback in real time.
  • Enhanced Sound Classifier: Automatically detects and adjusts to 11 distinct sound environments for smooth, personalized transitions.
  • Direct Streaming: Supports hands-free calls and high-quality audio streaming from both Apple and Android devices.
  • Accessories: Works with the Charge n Clean system for charging and automatic sanitizing, and with Widex TV Play™ 2 for superior TV audio streaming.
  • All-Day Power: Rechargeable battery offers up to 25 hours of use, including 7 hours of streaming on a full charge.

Widex SmartRIC

Widex SmartRIC is a new type of hearing aid made for better hearing in noisy places. It has a unique L-shape design that sits higher on the ear to help pick up speech more clearly. The battery lasts up to 37 hours, so you can use it all day without worry.

The portable charger holds enough power for a whole week, making it easy to take with you. The advanced microphone cover helps reduce background noise, making conversations clearer.

Key Features:

  • App: Works with the Moment app so you can adjust settings to fit your needs.
  • Bluetooth: Connects to both iPhone and Android devices for easy streaming.
  • Tinnitus Relief: Uses special Zen sounds to help manage ringing in the ears.

Widex Moment Sheer RIC

The Widex Moment Sheer RIC (receiver-in-the-canal) hearing aid has a sleek design with new microphone placement and a redesigned charger. The Moment app lets you customize your settings, and dual AI technology helps improve your hearing experience.

Widex Moment Sheer RIC can help people with mild to severe hearing loss. It comes with either a rechargeable or disposable battery. The battery lasts about 29 hours without streaming or 16 hours with 8 hours of streaming.

Key Features:

  • Bluetooth: Works with both iPhone and Android devices.
  • App: Allows you to adjust settings for a personalized fit.
  • Tinnitus Relief: Uses special Zen sounds to reduce tinnitus symptoms.

Widex Moment RIC

The Widex Moment RIC hearing aid is small and delivers natural sound. It has improved sound processing that makes speech clearer, even in noisy places. Studies show that 91% of users are happy with the sound quality, and five times more people preferred Moment for speech in noise compared to their old hearing aids.

Widex Moment RIC works for mild to severe hearing loss and comes with a rechargeable or disposable battery option.

Key Features:

  • Bluetooth: Connects to iPhones and Androids for streaming.
  • App: Lets you adjust settings for a better fit.
  • Tinnitus Relief: Zen programs help manage tinnitus with special sounds.

Widex Moment BTE

Widex Moment BTE (behind-the-ear) uses AI to learn what sounds you like. The SoundSense Learn feature helps adjust settings automatically based on what you need at the moment.

This model fits mild to severe hearing loss and comes with a rechargeable or size 13 disposable battery.

Key Features:

  • Bluetooth: Works with iPhones and Androids.
  • App: Adjusts settings based on your needs.
  • Tinnitus Relief: Zen sounds help with ringing in the ears.

Widex Moment CROS

Widex is known for its CROS technology, which helps people who can hear in only one ear. The Widex CROS sends sound from the ear that cannot hear to the ear that can. This helps with sound direction and hearing in noisy places.

It can be used as a CROS (for hearing loss in one ear) or a BiCROS (for hearing loss in both ears but worse in one). Widex CROS uses less power than other CROS devices, making it more efficient.

Widex Moment ITE

Widex Moment ITE (in-the-ear) hearing aids are custom-made to fit your ear perfectly. They automatically adjust to give clear and natural sound.

Widex Moment ITE is good for mild to moderate hearing loss. It comes with either size 10 or 312 disposable batteries, lasting between 105 and 150 hours, depending on use.

Key Features:

  • Custom Fit: Made to fit your ear perfectly.
  • Connectivity: Works with Bluetooth and remote control apps.
  • Tinnitus Relief: Zen programs help manage ringing in the ears.

Widex Assistive Listening Devices

  • Widex TV Play: Streams TV sound directly to your hearing aids for clear sound.
  • Widex Sound Assist: Works as a microphone, hands-free device, and remote control.
  • Com-Dex, RC-Dex, TV-Dex: Additional accessories for better hearing control.

Older Widex Hearing Aids Supported by Audiology & Hearing Centers

If you have an older Widex hearing aid, we may still be able to service or repair it. 

  • Widex Evoke
  • Widex Beyond
  • Widex Unique
  • Widex Dream

Please contact us for more details.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the best Widex hearing aid? 

The Widex Moment Sheer 440 is the top model. It has the best sound features, including wind noise reduction and speech enhancement.

What are the levels of Widex Moment? 

There are three levels of Widex Moment hearing aids:

  • Widex Moment 440: Premium Level
  • Widex Moment 330: Standard Level
  • Widex Moment 220: Basic Level

Is Widex Moment good for tinnitus? 

Yes! Widex Moment hearing aids come with Zen programs to help with tinnitus. These programs use relaxing sounds to make tinnitus less noticeable.

Find the Right Widex Hearing Aid for You

Audiology & Hearing Centers provides a wide selection of Widex hearing aids in Tampa Palms, Venice, Palm Harbor, Tampa Westchase, Sarasota, Bonita Springs FL and Alpharetta, GA.

Let our expert audiologists choose the best hearing solutions for your unique hearing needs.

  • Tampa Palms FL
  • Tampa Westchase, FL
  • Palm Harbor, FL
  • Venice, FL
  • Sarasota, FL
  • Bonita Springs, FL
  • Alpharetta, GA
